subject: Chemistry
question_html: The decomposition of NH3 on platinum surface is zero order reaction. What are the rates of production of N2 and H2 if k = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1?
question_text: The decomposition of NH 3 on platinum surface is zero order reaction. What are the rates of production of N 2 and H 2 if k = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 ?
explanation: {tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex} Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} For zero order reaction rate = k {tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex} Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} For zero order reaction rate = k {tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex}
Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
For zero order reaction rate = k
{tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex}
{tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 293 K to 313 K. Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction assuming that it does not change with temperature.
question_text: The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 293 K to 313 K. Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction assuming that it does not change with temperature.
explanation: From Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex} \log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{{\rm E}_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right){/tex} It is given that, {tex}{k_2} = 4{k_1}{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 293K; {T_2} = 313K{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\log \frac{{4{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{313 - 293}}{{293 \times 313}}} \right){/tex} {tex}0.6021 = \frac{{20 \times {E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{/tex} {tex}{E_a} = \frac{{0.6021 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{{20}}{/tex} {tex} = 52863.33\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Hence, the required energy of activation is {tex}52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}.{/tex}
solution: From Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex} \log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{{\rm E}_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right){/tex} It is given that, {tex}{k_2} = 4{k_1}{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 293K; {T_2} = 313K{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\log \frac{{4{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{313 - 293}}{{293 \times 313}}} \right){/tex} {tex}0.6021 = \frac{{20 \times {E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{/tex} {tex}{E_a} = \frac{{0.6021 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{{20}}{/tex} {tex} = 52863.33\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Hence, the required energy of activation is {tex}52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}.{/tex}
solution_html:
From Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex} \log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{{\rm E}_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right){/tex}
It is given that, {tex}{k_2} = 4{k_1}{/tex}
{tex}{T_1} = 293K; {T_2} = 313K{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\log \frac{{4{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{313 - 293}}{{293 \times 313}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}0.6021 = \frac{{20 \times {E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = \frac{{0.6021 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{{20}}{/tex}
{tex} = 52863.33\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Hence, the required energy of activation is {tex}52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}.{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The rate constant for a zero order reaction {tex}{A} \rightarrow {P}{/tex} is {tex}0.0030 {~mol} {~L}^{-1} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}. How long will it take for the initial concentration of {tex}A{/tex} to fall from 0.10 M to 0.075 M?
question_text: The rate constant for a zero order reaction {tex}{A} \rightarrow {P}{/tex} is {tex}0.0030 {~mol} {~L}^{-1} {~s}^{-1}{/tex} . How long will it take for the initial concentration of {tex}A{/tex} to fall from 0.10 M to 0.075 M?
explanation: For zero order reaction, rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{[R]_{0}-[R]}{t}{/tex} Given, [R] 0 = 0.10 M, [R] = 0.075 M and k = 0.0030 mol L -1 s -1 {tex}\therefore \quad t=\frac{[R]_{0}-[R]}{k}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{010-0.075}{0.0030}=833 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution: For zero order reaction, rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{[R]_{0}-[R]}{t}{/tex} Given, [R] 0 = 0.10 M, [R] = 0.075 M and k = 0.0030 mol L -1 s -1 {tex}\therefore \quad t=\frac{[R]_{0}-[R]}{k}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{010-0.075}{0.0030}=833 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution_html:
For zero order reaction,
rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{[R]_{0}-[R]}{t}{/tex}
Given, [R]0 = 0.10 M,
[R] = 0.075 M and
k = 0.0030 mol L-1 s-1
{tex}\therefore \quad t=\frac{[R]_{0}-[R]}{k}{/tex}
{tex}t=\frac{010-0.075}{0.0030}=833 \mathrm{s}{/tex}

question_html: A reaction is of second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is (i) doubled (ii) reduced to half?
question_text: A reaction is of second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is (i) doubled (ii) reduced to half?
explanation: Since rate = k[A] 2 Let [A] = a, then rate = ka 2 If [A] = 2a, then new rate = k(2a) 2 = 4ka 2 = 4 times If [A] = a/2, then new rate = k(a/2) 2 = k(a 2 /4) = 1/4 times
solution: Since rate = k[A] 2 Let [A] = a, then rate = ka 2 If [A] = 2a, then new rate = k(2a) 2 = 4ka 2 = 4 times If [A] = a/2, then new rate = k(a/2) 2 = k(a 2 /4) = 1/4 times
solution_html:
Since rate = k[A]2
Let [A] = a, then rate = ka2

  1. If [A] = 2a, then new rate = k(2a)2 = 4ka2 = 4 times
  2. If [A] = a/2, then new rate = k(a/2)2 = k(a2/4) = 1/4 times

question_html:
The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of N2O5 (g) at constant volume:
2N2O5 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2N2O4 (g) + O2 (g)

S. No. {tex}\frac {Time} {s}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\text{Total}}\,{\text{Pressure}}\,}}{{{\text{(atm)}}}}{/tex}
a.  0 0.5
b. 100 0.512

Calculate the rate constant.
question_text: The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of N 2 O 5 (g) at constant volume: 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2N 2 O 4 (g) + O 2 (g) S. No. {tex}\frac {Time} {s}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\text{Total}}\,{\text{Pressure}}\,}}{{{\text{(atm)}}}}{/tex} a. 0 0.5 b. 100 0.512 Calculate the rate constant.
explanation: Let the pressure of N 2 O 5 (g) decrease by 2x atm. As two moles of N 2 O 5 decomposes to give two moles of N 2 O 4 (g) and one mole of O 2 (g), the pressure of N 2 O 4 (g) increases by 2x atm and that of O 2 (g) increases by x atm. 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2N 2 O 4 (g) + O 2 (g) Start t = 0 0.5 atm 0 atm 0 atm At time t (0.5 - 2x) atm 2x atm x atm {tex}{{\text{P}}_{\text{t}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex} = (0.5 - 2x) + 2x + x = 0.5 + x x = p t - 0.5 {tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 0.5 - 2x = 0.5 - 2(p t - 0.5) = 1.5 - 2p t At t = 100 s; p t = 0.512 atm {tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 = 0.476 atm. Rate constant k = 4.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 s -1
solution: Let the pressure of N 2 O 5 (g) decrease by 2x atm. As two moles of N 2 O 5 decomposes to give two moles of N 2 O 4 (g) and one mole of O 2 (g), the pressure of N 2 O 4 (g) increases by 2x atm and that of O 2 (g) increases by x atm. 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2N 2 O 4 (g) + O 2 (g) Start t = 0 0.5 atm 0 atm 0 atm At time t (0.5 - 2x) atm 2x atm x atm {tex}{{\text{P}}_{\text{t}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex} = (0.5 - 2x) + 2x + x = 0.5 + x x = p t - 0.5 {tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 0.5 - 2x = 0.5 - 2(p t - 0.5) = 1.5 - 2p t At t = 100 s; p t = 0.512 atm {tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 = 0.476 atm. Rate constant k = 4.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 s -1
solution_html:
Let the pressure of N2O5 (g) decrease by 2x atm. As two moles of N2O5 decomposes to give two moles of N2O4 (g) and one mole of O2 (g), the pressure of N2O4 (g) increases by 2x atm and that of O2 (g) increases by x atm.

  2N2O5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2N2O4 (g)  +  O2 (g)
Start t = 0 0.5 atm   0 atm   0 atm
At time t (0.5 - 2x) atm   2x atm   x atm

{tex}{{\text{P}}_{\text{t}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex}
= (0.5 - 2x) + 2x + x = 0.5 + x
x = pt - 0.5
{tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 0.5 - 2x
= 0.5 - 2(pt - 0.5) = 1.5 - 2pt
At t = 100 s; pt = 0.512 atm
{tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 = 0.476 atm.
Rate constant k = 4.98{tex}\times{/tex}10-4s-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Why is molecularity applicable only for elementary reactions and order applicable for elementary as well as complex reaction?
question_text: Why is molecularity applicable only for elementary reactions and order applicable for elementary as well as complex reaction?
explanation: A complex reaction occurs through several elementary reactions gives us products. The numbers of molecules involved in each elementary reaction may be different i.e., the molecularity (reaction taking place in one step) of the step may be different. Therefore, the molecularity of the overall complex reaction is meaningless. On the other hand, the order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is, therefore, applicable even in the case of complex reactions.
solution: A complex reaction occurs through several elementary reactions gives us products. The numbers of molecules involved in each elementary reaction may be different i.e., the molecularity (reaction taking place in one step) of the step may be different. Therefore, the molecularity of the overall complex reaction is meaningless. On the other hand, the order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is, therefore, applicable even in the case of complex reactions.
solution_html: A complex reaction occurs through several elementary reactions gives us products. The numbers of molecules involved in each elementary reaction may be different i.e., the molecularity (reaction taking place in one step) of the step may be different. Therefore, the molecularity of the overall complex reaction is meaningless. On the other hand, the order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is, therefore, applicable even in the case of complex reactions.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For the reaction {tex}{A}+{B} \rightarrow{/tex} Products, the following initial rates were obtained at various initial concentrations of reactants:

Sl. No. {tex}[A] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}[B] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} Initial rate {tex}/ {mol} {L}^{-1} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}
1 0.1 0.1 0.05
2 0.2 0.1 0.10
3 0.1 0.2 0.05

Determine the order of the reaction with respect to A and B and overall order of the reaction.
question_text: For the reaction {tex}{A}+{B} \rightarrow{/tex} Products, the following initial rates were obtained at various initial concentrations of reactants: Sl. No. {tex}[A] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}[B] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} Initial rate {tex}/ {mol} {L}^{-1} {~s}^{-1}{/tex} 1 0.1 0.1 0.05 2 0.2 0.1 0.10 3 0.1 0.2 0.05 Determine the order of the reaction with respect to A and B and overall order of the reaction.
explanation: {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 1 \text { Rate }_1={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 2 \text { Rate }_2={k}(0.2)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=1.0 \times 10^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 3 \text { Rate }_3={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.2)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.1}{0.5}=\frac{k \times 0.2^x \times 0.1^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {x}=1 {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.05}{0.05}=\frac{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.2^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {y}=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^1[{~B}]^0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Overall order }=1 {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 1 \text { Rate }_1={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 2 \text { Rate }_2={k}(0.2)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=1.0 \times 10^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 3 \text { Rate }_3={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.2)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.1}{0.5}=\frac{k \times 0.2^x \times 0.1^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {x}=1 {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.05}{0.05}=\frac{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.2^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {y}=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^1[{~B}]^0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Overall order }=1 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Eq. } 1 \text { Rate }_1={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Eq. } 2 \text { Rate }_2={k}(0.2)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=1.0 \times 10^{-1} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Eq. } 3 \text { Rate }_3={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.2)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{0.1}{0.5}=\frac{k \times 0.2^x \times 0.1^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Hence } {x}=1 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{0.05}{0.05}=\frac{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.2^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Hence } {y}=0 {/tex}
{tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^1[{~B}]^0 {/tex}
{tex} \text { Overall order }=1 {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A certain reaction is {tex}50 \%{/tex} complete in 20 minutes at 300 K and the same reaction is {tex}50 \%{/tex} complete in 5 minutes at 350 K. Calculate the activation energy if it is a first order reaction.
{tex}\left[{R}=8.314 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}\right.{/tex}; {tex}\left.\log 4=0.602\right]{/tex}
question_text: A certain reaction is {tex}50 \%{/tex} complete in 20 minutes at 300 K and the same reaction is {tex}50 \%{/tex} complete in 5 minutes at 350 K. Calculate the activation energy if it is a first order reaction. {tex}\left[{R}=8.314 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}\right.{/tex} ; {tex}\left.\log 4=0.602\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{k} {/tex}
{tex} {k}_1=\frac{0.693}{20}=0.03465 / 3.465 \times 10^{-2} {~min}^{-1} {/tex}
{tex} {k}_2=\frac{0.693}{5}=0.1386 / 1.386 \times 10^{-1} {~min}^{-1} {/tex}
{tex} \log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\frac{E_{{a}}}{2.303 {R}}\left[\frac{T_2-T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \log \frac{0.1386}{0.03465}=\frac{E a}{2.303 \times 8.314} \frac{[350-300]}{[350 \times 300]} {/tex}
{tex} \log 4=\frac{E a}{19.15} \frac{[50]}{[350 \times 300]} {/tex}
{tex} {Ea}=24209 {~J} {~mol}^{-1} \text { or } 24.209 {~kJ} {~mol}^{-1} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The rate of a chemical reaction is expressed either in terms of decrease in the concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of a product per unit time. Rate of the reaction depends upon the nature of reactants, concentration of reactants, temperature, presence of catalyst, surface area of the reactants and presence of light. Rate of reaction is directly related to the concentration of reactant. Rate law states that the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration terms on which the rate of reaction actually depends, as observed experimentally. The sum of powers of the concentration of the reactants in the Rate law expression is called order of reaction while the number of reacting species taking part in an elementary reaction which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of the reaction.
Answer the following questions:

    1. What is a rate determining step? (1)
    2. Define complex reaction. (1)
  2. What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction? (1)

    OR

    Why is molecularity applicable only for elementary reactions whereas order is applicable for elementary as well as complex reactions? (1)
  3. The conversion of molecule X to Y follows second order kinetics. If concentration of {tex}X{/tex} is increased 3 times, how will it affect the rate of formation of Y? (1)
question_text: The rate of a chemical reaction is expressed either in terms of decrease in the concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of a product per unit time. Rate of the reaction depends upon the nature of reactants, concentration of reactants, temperature, presence of catalyst, surface area of the reactants and presence of light. Rate of reaction is directly related to the concentration of reactant. Rate law states that the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration terms on which the rate of reaction actually depends, as observed experimentally. The sum of powers of the concentration of the reactants in the Rate law expression is called order of reaction while the number of reacting species taking part in an elementary reaction which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of the reaction. Answer the following questions: What is a rate determining step? (1) Define complex reaction. (1) What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction? (1) OR Why is molecularity applicable only for elementary reactions whereas order is applicable for elementary as well as complex reactions? (1) The conversion of molecule X to Y follows second order kinetics. If concentration of {tex}X{/tex} is increased 3 times, how will it affect the rate of formation of Y? (1)
explanation: Slowest step. Series of elementary reactions / Reactions involving two or more steps. Increases with an increase in temperature. OR Molecularity is defined only for elementary reactions whereas order is experimentally determined hence applicable for both/ Because molecularity of each elementary reaction in complex reaction may be different and hence meaningless for overall complex reaction whereas order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is therefore applicable for both. 9 times
solution: Slowest step. Series of elementary reactions / Reactions involving two or more steps. Increases with an increase in temperature. OR Molecularity is defined only for elementary reactions whereas order is experimentally determined hence applicable for both/ Because molecularity of each elementary reaction in complex reaction may be different and hence meaningless for overall complex reaction whereas order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is therefore applicable for both. 9 times
solution_html:
1. Slowest step.
    2. Series of elementary reactions / Reactions involving two or more steps.
  2. Increases with an increase in temperature.

    OR

    Molecularity is defined only for elementary reactions whereas order is experimentally determined hence applicable for both/ Because molecularity of each elementary reaction in complex reaction may be different and hence meaningless for overall complex reaction whereas order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is therefore applicable for both.
  3. 9 times

question_html:
The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of {tex}{N}_{2} {O}_{5}({~g}){/tex} at constant volume:
{tex}2 {~N}_{2} {O}_{5}({~g}) \longrightarrow 2 {~N}_{2} {O}_{4}({~g})+{O}_{2}({~g}){/tex}
Calculate rate constant.

S. No. Time/s Total Pressure/atm
1 0 0.5
2 100 0.625

[Given: {tex}\log 2=0 \cdot 3010, \log 10=1{/tex}]
question_text: The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of {tex}{N}_{2} {O}_{5}({~g}){/tex} at constant volume: {tex}2 {~N}_{2} {O}_{5}({~g}) \longrightarrow 2 {~N}_{2} {O}_{4}({~g})+{O}_{2}({~g}){/tex} Calculate rate constant. S. No. Time/s Total Pressure/atm 1 0 0.5 2 100 0.625 [Given: {tex}\log 2=0 \cdot 3010, \log 10=1{/tex} ]
solution_html:
{tex}{P}_{{t}}={P}_{{i}}-2 {x}+2 {x}+{x}={P}_{{i}}+{x}{/tex}
{tex}{x}={P}_{{t}}-{P}_{{i}}{/tex}
{tex}{p}_{{A}}={P}_{{i}}-2 {x}{/tex}
{tex}={P}_{{i}}-2\left({P}_{{t}}-{P}_{{i}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=3 {P}_{{i}}-2 {P}_{{t}}{/tex}
{tex}{k}=\frac{2.303}{{t}} \log \frac{{P}_{{l}}}{{P}_{{A}}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Where pi }=0.5 {~atm},{/tex}
{tex}{p}_{{A}} =3 {p}_{{i}}-2 {p}_{{t}}{/tex}
{tex}=(3 \times 0.5)-(2 \times 0.625){/tex}
{tex}=0.25 {~atm}{/tex}
{tex}{k}=\frac{2.303}{100 {~s}} \log \frac{0.5 {~atm}}{0.25 {~atm}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{100 {~s}} \times 0.3010{/tex}
{tex}=6.93 \times 10^{-3} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The rate of the chemical reaction doubles for an increase of 10 K in absolute temperature from 298 K. Calculate activation energy ({tex}{E}_{{a}}{/tex}).
    {tex}\left[2 \cdot 303 {R}=19 \cdot 15 {JK}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}, \log 2=0 \cdot 3\right]{/tex}
  2. For a reaction:
    {tex}2 {H}_{2} {O}_{2} \xrightarrow{{I}^{-}} 2 {H}_{2} {O}+{O}_{2}{/tex}
    the proposed mechanism is as given below:
    1. {tex}{H}_{2} {O}_{2}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {H}_{2} {O}+{IO}^{-}{/tex} (slow)
    2. {tex}{H}_{2} {O}_{2}+{IO}^{-} \longrightarrow {H}_{2} {O}+{I}^{-}+{O}_{2}{/tex} (fast)
      1. Write rate law for the reaction.
      2. Write the overall order and molecularity of the reaction.
question_text: The rate of the chemical reaction doubles for an increase of 10 K in absolute temperature from 298 K. Calculate activation energy ( {tex}{E}_{{a}}{/tex} ). {tex}\left[2 \cdot 303 {R}=19 \cdot 15 {JK}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}, \log 2=0 \cdot 3\right]{/tex} For a reaction: {tex}2 {H}_{2} {O}_{2} \xrightarrow{{I}^{-}} 2 {H}_{2} {O}+{O}_{2}{/tex} the proposed mechanism is as given below: {tex}{H}_{2} {O}_{2}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {H}_{2} {O}+{IO}^{-}{/tex} (slow) {tex}{H}_{2} {O}_{2}+{IO}^{-} \longrightarrow {H}_{2} {O}+{I}^{-}+{O}_{2}{/tex} (fast) Write rate law for the reaction. Write the overall order and molecularity of the reaction.
explanation: {tex} \log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{2 \cdot 303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right] {/tex} {tex} \log \frac{2 k_1}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{1}{298}-\frac{1}{308}\right] {/tex} {tex} 0 \cdot 3=\frac{E_a}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{10}{298 \times 308}\right] {/tex} {tex} E_a=\frac{0 \cdot 3 \times 19 \cdot 15 \times 298 \times 308}{10} {/tex} {tex} E_a=52729 {Jmol}^{-1} \text { or } 52.729 {kJmol}^{-1} {/tex} Rate {tex}={k}\left[{H}_2 {O}_2\right]\left[{I}^{-}\right]{/tex} Overall order: 2/Second Molecularity: 2/Bimolecular

question_html:
By giving reasons, explain how the rate of the reaction for a given reaction will be affected when:

  1. the temperature at which the reaction was taking place is decreased.
  2. a catalyst is added.
question_text: By giving reasons, explain how the rate of the reaction for a given reaction will be affected when: the temperature at which the reaction was taking place is decreased. a catalyst is added.
explanation: Rate of reaction decreases on decreasing temperature. Effective collisions decrease/Decrease in fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than {tex}{E}_{{a}}{/tex} . Rate of reaction increases on adding a catalyst. Due to lowering of activation energy.
solution: Rate of reaction decreases on decreasing temperature. Effective collisions decrease/Decrease in fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than {tex}{E}_{{a}}{/tex} . Rate of reaction increases on adding a catalyst. Due to lowering of activation energy.
solution_html:
• Rate of reaction decreases on decreasing temperature.
    • Effective collisions decrease/Decrease in fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than {tex}{E}_{{a}}{/tex}.
    • Rate of reaction increases on adding a catalyst.
    • Due to lowering of activation energy.

question_html:
The following data were obtained during the kinetic studies of the reaction:
{tex}A+2 B \rightarrow C+2 D{/tex}

Experiment Initial [A] mol/L） Initial [B] (mol/L) Initial rate of formation of {tex}{C}\left({M} {min}^{-1}\right){/tex}
1 {tex}0 .10{/tex} 0.10 {tex}3.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
2 {tex}0 .30{/tex} 0.30 {tex}9.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
3 {tex}0 .10{/tex} {tex}0 . 30{/tex} {tex}3.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
4 0.20 0.40 {tex}6.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}

Determine the order of reaction with respect to each reactant and the overall order of the reaction. Write the rate law expression for the reaction.
question_text: The following data were obtained during the kinetic studies of the reaction: {tex}A+2 B \rightarrow C+2 D{/tex} Experiment Initial [A] mol/L） Initial [B] (mol/L) Initial rate of formation of {tex}{C}\left({M} {min}^{-1}\right){/tex} 1 {tex}0 .10{/tex} 0.10 {tex}3.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} 2 {tex}0 .30{/tex} 0.30 {tex}9.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} 3 {tex}0 .10{/tex} {tex}0 . 30{/tex} {tex}3.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} 4 0.20 0.40 {tex}6.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} Determine the order of reaction with respect to each reactant and the overall order of the reaction. Write the rate law expression for the reaction.
explanation: Let the order w.r.t to A be x and y w.r.t to B {tex}\text { Rate } ={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}}{/tex} {tex}3 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.10]^{{x}}[0.10]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}9 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.30]^{{x}}[0.30]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(2) {tex}3 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.10]^{{x}}[0.30]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(3) {tex}6 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.20]^{{x}}[0.40]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(4) Dividing Exp. (1) by (3) {tex}{y}=0{/tex} , the order w.r.t B is 0. Dividing Exp. (2) by (3) {tex}3=3^{x}{/tex} i.e., {tex}{x}=1{/tex} , order w.r.t A is 1. Overall order of the reaction {tex}=1{/tex} {tex}\text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{1}[{~B}]^{0} / \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]{/tex}
solution: Let the order w.r.t to A be x and y w.r.t to B {tex}\text { Rate } ={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}}{/tex} {tex}3 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.10]^{{x}}[0.10]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}9 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.30]^{{x}}[0.30]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(2) {tex}3 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.10]^{{x}}[0.30]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(3) {tex}6 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.20]^{{x}}[0.40]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(4) Dividing Exp. (1) by (3) {tex}{y}=0{/tex} , the order w.r.t B is 0. Dividing Exp. (2) by (3) {tex}3=3^{x}{/tex} i.e., {tex}{x}=1{/tex} , order w.r.t A is 1. Overall order of the reaction {tex}=1{/tex} {tex}\text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{1}[{~B}]^{0} / \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the order w.r.t to A be x and y w.r.t to B
{tex}\text { Rate } ={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}}{/tex}
{tex}3 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.10]^{{x}}[0.10]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex}9 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.30]^{{x}}[0.30]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(2)
{tex}3 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.10]^{{x}}[0.30]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(3)
{tex}6 \times 10^{-4} ={k}[0.20]^{{x}}[0.40]^{{y}}{/tex} ...(4)
Dividing Exp. (1) by (3)
{tex}{y}=0{/tex}, the order w.r.t B is 0.
Dividing Exp. (2) by (3)
{tex}3=3^{x}{/tex}
i.e., {tex}{x}=1{/tex}, order w.r.t A is 1.
Overall order of the reaction {tex}=1{/tex}
{tex}\text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{1}[{~B}]^{0} / \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]{/tex}

Experiment {tex}[{A}] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}[{~B}] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} Initial Rate/mol L-1 min-1
1 0.01 0.01 5{tex}\times{/tex}10-3
2 0.02 0.01 1{tex}\times{/tex}10-2

Calculate the order of the reaction with respect to A and B. Determine the rate constant of the reaction.
question_text: The rate of a reaction: {tex}{A}+{B} \longrightarrow{/tex} product is given below as a function of different initial concentrations of {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} . Experiment {tex}[{A}] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}[{~B}] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} Initial Rate/mol L -1 min -1 1 0.01 0.01 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 2 0.02 0.01 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 3 0.01 0.02 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 Calculate the order of the reaction with respect to A and B. Determine the rate constant of the reaction.
explanation: Rate {tex}=k[A]^{p}[B]^{q}{/tex} {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-3}={k}[0.01]^{{p}}[0.01]^{{q}} \ldots{/tex} Eq 1 {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-2}=k[0.02]^{p}[0.01]^{q} \ldots{/tex} . Eq 2 {tex}5 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-3}={k}[0.01]^{{p}}[0.02]^{{q}} \ldots{/tex} Eq 3 On Comparing (eq1) and (eq3) {tex}1 =(2)^{q}{/tex} {tex}q =0{/tex} On Comparing (eq1) and (eq2) {tex}(2)^{1}=(2)^{p}{/tex} {tex}p=1{/tex} {tex}\text { Order } w \cdot r \cdot t \ A=1{/tex} Order {tex}w \cdot r \cdot t \ B=0{/tex} From eq 1 {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-3}=k[0.01]^{1}[0.01]^{0}{/tex} {tex}k=0.5 {~min}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: Rate {tex}=k[A]^{p}[B]^{q}{/tex} {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-3}={k}[0.01]^{{p}}[0.01]^{{q}} \ldots{/tex} Eq 1 {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-2}=k[0.02]^{p}[0.01]^{q} \ldots{/tex} . Eq 2 {tex}5 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-3}={k}[0.01]^{{p}}[0.02]^{{q}} \ldots{/tex} Eq 3 On Comparing (eq1) and (eq3) {tex}1 =(2)^{q}{/tex} {tex}q =0{/tex} On Comparing (eq1) and (eq2) {tex}(2)^{1}=(2)^{p}{/tex} {tex}p=1{/tex} {tex}\text { Order } w \cdot r \cdot t \ A=1{/tex} Order {tex}w \cdot r \cdot t \ B=0{/tex} From eq 1 {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-3}=k[0.01]^{1}[0.01]^{0}{/tex} {tex}k=0.5 {~min}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Rate {tex}=k[A]^{p}[B]^{q}{/tex}
{tex}5.0 \times 10^{-3}={k}[0.01]^{{p}}[0.01]^{{q}} \ldots{/tex} Eq 1
{tex}1.0 \times 10^{-2}=k[0.02]^{p}[0.01]^{q} \ldots{/tex}. Eq 2
{tex}5 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-3}={k}[0.01]^{{p}}[0.02]^{{q}} \ldots{/tex} Eq 3
On Comparing (eq1) and (eq3)
{tex}1 =(2)^{q}{/tex}
{tex}q =0{/tex}
On Comparing (eq1) and (eq2)
{tex}(2)^{1}=(2)^{p}{/tex}
{tex}p=1{/tex}
{tex}\text { Order } w \cdot r \cdot t \ A=1{/tex}
Order {tex}w \cdot r \cdot t \ B=0{/tex}
From eq 1
{tex}5.0 \times 10^{-3}=k[0.01]^{1}[0.01]^{0}{/tex}
{tex}k=0.5 {~min}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Write two differences between order of reaction and molecularity of reaction.
question_text: Write two differences between order of reaction and molecularity of reaction.
explanation: Molecularity of a reaction Order of reaction The number of reacting species which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of a reaction. The sum of powers of the concentrations of the reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. Molecularityis always a whole number value. Order may have zero, whole number, negative or fractional, values.
solution: Molecularity of a reaction Order of reaction The number of reacting species which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of a reaction. The sum of powers of the concentrations of the reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. Molecularityis always a whole number value. Order may have zero, whole number, negative or fractional, values.
solution_html:
Molecularity of a reaction Order of reaction
The number of reacting species which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of a reaction. The sum of powers of the concentrations of the reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction.
Molecularityis always a whole number value. Order may have zero, whole number, negative or fractional, values.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A first order reaction has a rate constant 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 s-1. How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 2.5 g?
[log 2 = 0.301, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021]
question_text: A first order reaction has a rate constant 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 s -1 . How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 2.5 g? [log 2 = 0.301, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021]
explanation: {tex} \mathrm{k}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{\mathrm{o}}}{[\mathrm{R}]} {/tex} {tex} 1.25 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \left(\frac{5}{2 \cdot 5}\right) {/tex} {tex} \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3}} \log 2 {/tex} {tex} \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \cdot 303 \times 0 \cdot 301}{1 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} t = 554.5 s or 5.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 s
solution: {tex} \mathrm{k}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{\mathrm{o}}}{[\mathrm{R}]} {/tex} {tex} 1.25 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \left(\frac{5}{2 \cdot 5}\right) {/tex} {tex} \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3}} \log 2 {/tex} {tex} \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \cdot 303 \times 0 \cdot 301}{1 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} t = 554.5 s or 5.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 s
solution_html:
{tex} \mathrm{k}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{\mathrm{o}}}{[\mathrm{R}]} {/tex}
{tex} 1.25 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \left(\frac{5}{2 \cdot 5}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3}} \log 2 {/tex}
{tex} \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \cdot 303 \times 0 \cdot 301}{1 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
t = 554.5 s or 5.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 s

question_html:
The rate of a gaseous reaction triples when temperature is increased from 17oC to 27oC. Calculate the energy of activation for this reaction.
[Given: 2.303 R = 19.15 JK-1 mol-1, log 3 = 0.48]
question_text: The rate of a gaseous reaction triples when temperature is increased from 17 o C to 27 o C. Calculate the energy of activation for this reaction. [Given: 2.303 R = 19.15 JK -1 mol -1 , log 3 = 0.48]
explanation: {tex}\log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{2 \cdot 303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{E_a}{2 \cdot 303 R}\left[\frac{\mathrm{T} 2-\mathrm{T} 1}{T_1 T 2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{3 k_1}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{1}{290}-\frac{1}{300}\right]{/tex} 0.48 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{10}{290 \times 300}\right]{/tex} E a = {tex}\frac{0 \cdot 48 \times 19 \cdot 15 \times 290 \times 300}{10}{/tex} E a = 79970 Jmol -1 or 79.970 kJmol -1
solution: {tex}\log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{2 \cdot 303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{E_a}{2 \cdot 303 R}\left[\frac{\mathrm{T} 2-\mathrm{T} 1}{T_1 T 2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{3 k_1}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{1}{290}-\frac{1}{300}\right]{/tex} 0.48 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{10}{290 \times 300}\right]{/tex} E a = {tex}\frac{0 \cdot 48 \times 19 \cdot 15 \times 290 \times 300}{10}{/tex} E a = 79970 Jmol -1 or 79.970 kJmol -1
solution_html:
{tex}\log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{2 \cdot 303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{E_a}{2 \cdot 303 R}\left[\frac{\mathrm{T} 2-\mathrm{T} 1}{T_1 T 2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{3 k_1}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{1}{290}-\frac{1}{300}\right]{/tex}
0.48 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{19 \cdot 15}\left[\frac{10}{290 \times 300}\right]{/tex}
Ea = {tex}\frac{0 \cdot 48 \times 19 \cdot 15 \times 290 \times 300}{10}{/tex}
Ea = 79970 Jmol-1 or 79.970 kJmol-1

question_html:
The following initial rate data were obtained for the reaction:
2NO(g) + Br2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NOBr(g)

Expt. No. [NO]/mol L-1 [Br2]/mol L-1 Initial Rate (mol L-1 s-1)
1 0.05 0.05 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
2 0.05 0.15 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
3 0.15 0.05 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
  1. What is the order with respect to NO and Br2 in the reaction?
  2. Calculate the rate constant (k).
  3. Determine the rate of reaction when concentration of NO and Br2 are 0.4 M and 0.2 M, respectively.
question_text: The following initial rate data were obtained for the reaction: 2NO(g) + Br 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NOBr(g) Expt. No. [NO]/mol L -1 [Br 2 ]/mol L -1 Initial Rate (mol L -1 s -1 ) 1 0.05 0.05 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 2 0.05 0.15 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 3 0.15 0.05 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 What is the order with respect to NO and Br 2 in the reaction? Calculate the rate constant (k). Determine the rate of reaction when concentration of NO and Br 2 are 0.4 M and 0.2 M, respectively.
explanation: Rate = {tex}\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{NO}]^{\mathrm{p}}\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right]^{\mathrm{q}}{/tex} 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k[0.05] p [0.05] q ...(i) 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k[0.05] p [0.15] q ...(ii) 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k[0.15] p [0.05] q ...(iii) On Comparing (eq. i) and (eq. ii) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^\text q{/tex} q = 1 (eq. i) {tex}\div{/tex} (eq. iii) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^p{/tex} p = 2 Order w·r·t NO = 2 Order w·r·t Br 2 = 1 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k (0.05) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05 k = {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05 \times 0.05 \times 0.05}{/tex} k = 8 L 2 mol -2 s -1 (Unit can be ignored) Rate = k[NO] 2 [Br 2 ] = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.4) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.2) = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 mol L -1
solution: Rate = {tex}\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{NO}]^{\mathrm{p}}\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right]^{\mathrm{q}}{/tex} 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k[0.05] p [0.05] q ...(i) 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k[0.05] p [0.15] q ...(ii) 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k[0.15] p [0.05] q ...(iii) On Comparing (eq. i) and (eq. ii) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^\text q{/tex} q = 1 (eq. i) {tex}\div{/tex} (eq. iii) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^p{/tex} p = 2 Order w·r·t NO = 2 Order w·r·t Br 2 = 1 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k (0.05) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05 k = {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05 \times 0.05 \times 0.05}{/tex} k = 8 L 2 mol -2 s -1 (Unit can be ignored) Rate = k[NO] 2 [Br 2 ] = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.4) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.2) = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 mol L -1
solution_html:
Rate = {tex}\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{NO}]^{\mathrm{p}}\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right]^{\mathrm{q}}{/tex}
1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[0.05]p [0.05]q ...(i)
3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[0.05]p [0.15]q ...(ii)
9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[0.15]p [0.05]q ...(iii)
On Comparing (eq. i) and (eq. ii)
{tex}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^\text q{/tex}
q = 1
(eq. i) {tex}\div{/tex} (eq. iii)
{tex}\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^p{/tex}
p = 2

  1. Order w·r·t NO = 2
    Order w·r·t Br2 = 1
  2. 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k (0.05)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05
    k = {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05 \times 0.05 \times 0.05}{/tex}
    k = 8 L2 mol-2 s-1 (Unit can be ignored)
  3. Rate = k[NO]2 [Br2]
    = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.4)2 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.2)
    = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 mol L-1

question_text: A first-order reaction is 25% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the value of rate constant. In what time will the reaction be 80% complete? [Given: log 2 = 0.30, log 3 = 0.48, log 4 = 0.60, log 5 = 0.69]
explanation: {tex} {k}=\frac{2.303}{t} 1og \frac {[{R}]_t}{[{R}]} {/tex} {tex} k=\frac{2.303}{40 \min } \log \frac{4}{3} {/tex} {tex} k=\frac{2.30 .3}{40 {~min}} \times 0.12 {/tex} = 0.0069 min -1 For 80% completion, time required is, {tex} t =\frac{2.303}{0.0069} \log 5 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2.303 \times 0.69}{0.0069} {/tex} = 230.3 min
solution: {tex} {k}=\frac{2.303}{t} 1og \frac {[{R}]_t}{[{R}]} {/tex} {tex} k=\frac{2.303}{40 \min } \log \frac{4}{3} {/tex} {tex} k=\frac{2.30 .3}{40 {~min}} \times 0.12 {/tex} = 0.0069 min -1 For 80% completion, time required is, {tex} t =\frac{2.303}{0.0069} \log 5 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2.303 \times 0.69}{0.0069} {/tex} = 230.3 min

question_text: In a reaction, if the concentration of reactant X is tripled, the rate of reaction becomes twenty-seven times. What is the order of the reaction? State a condition under which a bimolecular reaction is kinetically a first-order reaction. Give an example of such a reaction.
explanation: Rate = k[X] p 27 Rate = k[3X] p {tex}\therefore{/tex} 27 = 3 p (3) 3 = 3 p p = 3 Third order When one of the reactants is in excess. Example: Hydrolysis of ester/ sucrose
solution: Rate = k[X] p 27 Rate = k[3X] p {tex}\therefore{/tex} 27 = 3 p (3) 3 = 3 p p = 3 Third order When one of the reactants is in excess. Example: Hydrolysis of ester/ sucrose

    p = 3 Third order
  2. When one of the reactants is in excess.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: For a first order reaction, show that time required for 99% completion is twice than the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction.
question_text: For a first order reaction, show that time required for 99% completion is twice than the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction.
explanation: For first order reaction, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex} where k = rate constant, a = initial amount of reactant, a - x = amount of reactant left after time t 99% completion means that x = 99 % of a = 0.99 a {tex}t_{99\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.99a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log {10^2} = 2 \times \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} Now for 90% completion: x = 90% of a = 0.90 a {tex}t_{90\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.90a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 10 = \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{t_{99\% }}}}{{{t_{90\% }}}} = \left( {\frac{{2 \times 2.303}}{k}} \right)/\frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} t 99% {tex} = 2 \times t_{90\% }{/tex}
solution: For first order reaction, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex} where k = rate constant, a = initial amount of reactant, a - x = amount of reactant left after time t 99% completion means that x = 99 % of a = 0.99 a {tex}t_{99\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.99a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log {10^2} = 2 \times \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} Now for 90% completion: x = 90% of a = 0.90 a {tex}t_{90\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.90a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 10 = \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{t_{99\% }}}}{{{t_{90\% }}}} = \left( {\frac{{2 \times 2.303}}{k}} \right)/\frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} t 99% {tex} = 2 \times t_{90\% }{/tex}
solution_html:
For first order reaction, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex}

where k = rate constant, a = initial amount of reactant, a - x = amount of reactant left after time t
99% completion means that
x = 99 % of a = 0.99 a
{tex}t_{99\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.99a}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log {10^2} = 2 \times \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex}
Now for  90% completion:
x = 90% of a = 0.90 a
{tex}t_{90\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.90a}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 10 = \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{t_{99\% }}}}{{{t_{90\% }}}} = \left( {\frac{{2 \times 2.303}}{k}} \right)/\frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex}
 t99% {tex} = 2 \times t_{90\% }{/tex}

{tex} {k}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{t}} \log \frac{[{R}]_0}{[{R}]} {/tex}
For 99.9 % completion
{tex} \text { Let }[R]_0=100 {/tex}
{tex} {[R]=100-99.9 \%=0.1} {/tex}
{tex} {t}_{99.9 \%}=\frac{2.303}{{k}} \log \frac{100}{0.1} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{k}} \log 1000 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{k}} \times 3 {/tex} ...(i)
Let {tex}[{R}]_0=100,[{R}]=100-50=50{/tex}
{tex} {t}_{50 \%}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{k}} \log \frac{100}{50} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{k}} \log 2 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2.303}{{k}} \times 0.3010 {/tex} ...(ii)
Divide (i) by (ii)
{tex} \frac{t_{99.9 \%}}{t_{50 \%}}=\frac{\frac{2.303}{k} \times 3}{\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{t_{99.9 \%}}{t_{50 \%}}=10 {/tex}
or {tex}{t}_{99 \cdot 9 \%}=10 {t}_{50 \%}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The rate constant of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 700 K to 720 K. Calculate the activation energy for this reaction.
{tex}[\log 2=0.30, \log 4=0.60,2.303 {R}{/tex} {tex}=19.15 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}]{/tex}
question_text: The rate constant of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 700 K to 720 K. Calculate the activation energy for this reaction. {tex}[\log 2=0.30, \log 4=0.60,2.303 {R}{/tex} {tex}=19.15 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}]{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 {R}}\left(\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex} It is given that, {tex}k_{2}=4 k_{1}{/tex} {tex} \text { Therefore, } \log \frac{4 k_1}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{19.15}\left(\frac{720-700}{700 \times 720}\right) {/tex} {tex} 0.60=\frac{20 \times E_a}{19.15 \times 700 \times 720} {/tex} {tex} E_a =\frac{0.60 \times 19.15 \times 700 \times 720}{20} {/tex} {tex} =289548 {~J} {~mol}^{-1} \text { or } {/tex} {tex} =289.5 {~kJ} {~mol}^{-1} {/tex}
solution: {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 {R}}\left(\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex} It is given that, {tex}k_{2}=4 k_{1}{/tex} {tex} \text { Therefore, } \log \frac{4 k_1}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{19.15}\left(\frac{720-700}{700 \times 720}\right) {/tex} {tex} 0.60=\frac{20 \times E_a}{19.15 \times 700 \times 720} {/tex} {tex} E_a =\frac{0.60 \times 19.15 \times 700 \times 720}{20} {/tex} {tex} =289548 {~J} {~mol}^{-1} \text { or } {/tex} {tex} =289.5 {~kJ} {~mol}^{-1} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 {R}}\left(\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex}
It is given that, {tex}k_{2}=4 k_{1}{/tex}
{tex} \text { Therefore, } \log \frac{4 k_1}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{19.15}\left(\frac{720-700}{700 \times 720}\right) {/tex}
{tex} 0.60=\frac{20 \times E_a}{19.15 \times 700 \times 720} {/tex}
{tex} E_a =\frac{0.60 \times 19.15 \times 700 \times 720}{20} {/tex}
{tex} =289548 {~J} {~mol}^{-1} \text { or } {/tex}
{tex} =289.5 {~kJ} {~mol}^{-1} {/tex}

question_text: A first order reaction is 50% complete in 30 minutes at 300 K and in 10 minutes at 320 K. Calculate activation energy (E a ) for the reaction. [R = 8.314 J K -1 mol -1 ] [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021]
explanation: {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t}{/tex} {tex}k_1=\frac{0.693}{30}{/tex} min -1 {tex}k_2=\frac{0.693}{10}{/tex} min -1 log {tex}\frac{\mathbf{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2 \cdot 303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex} log 3 = {tex}\frac{\text { Ea }}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{320}\right]{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E_a}=\frac{0.4771 \times 19 \cdot 147 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} Activation energy = E a = 43848 J mol -1 or 43.848 KJ mol -1 or 43.85 k J mol -1
solution: {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t}{/tex} {tex}k_1=\frac{0.693}{30}{/tex} min -1 {tex}k_2=\frac{0.693}{10}{/tex} min -1 log {tex}\frac{\mathbf{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2 \cdot 303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex} log 3 = {tex}\frac{\text { Ea }}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{320}\right]{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E_a}=\frac{0.4771 \times 19 \cdot 147 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} Activation energy = E a = 43848 J mol -1 or 43.848 KJ mol -1 or 43.85 k J mol -1
solution_html:
{tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t}{/tex}
{tex}k_1=\frac{0.693}{30}{/tex} min-1
{tex}k_2=\frac{0.693}{10}{/tex} min-1
log{tex}\frac{\mathbf{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2 \cdot 303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex}
log 3 = {tex}\frac{\text { Ea }}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{320}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{E_a}=\frac{0.4771 \times 19 \cdot 147 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex}
Activation energy = Ea = 43848 J mol-1 or 43.848 KJ mol-1 or 43.85 k J mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A chemical reaction
2N2O5(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 4NO2(g) + O2(g)
in gas phase was carried out in a closed vessel. The concentration of NO2 was found to increase by 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 in 10 seconds. Calculate:

  1. the rate of formation of NO2, and
  2. the rate of consumption of N2O5.
question_text: A chemical reaction 2N 2 O 5(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 4NO 2(g) + O 2(g) in gas phase was carried out in a closed vessel. The concentration of NO 2 was found to increase by 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 mol L -1 in 10 seconds. Calculate: the rate of formation of NO 2 , and the rate of consumption of N 2 O 5 .
explanation: {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right)}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{1}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} Rate of formation of NO 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10}{/tex} mol L -1 = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 Rate of consumption of N 2 O 5 {tex}-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{2}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 mol L -1 s -1 = 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 OR 6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 mol L -1 s -1
solution: {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right)}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{1}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} Rate of formation of NO 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10}{/tex} mol L -1 = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 Rate of consumption of N 2 O 5 {tex}-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{2}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 mol L -1 s -1 = 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 OR 6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 mol L -1 s -1
solution_html:
{tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right)}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{1}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}

  1. Rate of formation of NO2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10}{/tex} mol L-1 = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1
  2. Rate of consumption of N2O5{tex}-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{2}{4} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 mol L-1 s-1
    = 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1 OR 6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 mol L-1 s-1

(R = 8.314 J K-1 mol-1)
(Given : log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021)
question_text: The rate of a reaction doubles when temperature changes from 27 o C to 37 o C. Calculate energy of activation for the reaction. (R = 8.314 J K -1 mol -1 ) (Given : log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021)
explanation: log {tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2 \cdot 303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex} log {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{E a}{2 \cdot 303 \times 8 \cdot 314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{310}\right]{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{0 \cdot 3010 \times 19 \cdot 147 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \times 310}{10}{/tex} Activation energy=E a = 53598.2 J mol -1 or 53.598 kJ mol -1 or 53.6 kJ mol -1
solution: log {tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2 \cdot 303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex} log {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{E a}{2 \cdot 303 \times 8 \cdot 314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{310}\right]{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{0 \cdot 3010 \times 19 \cdot 147 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \times 310}{10}{/tex} Activation energy=E a = 53598.2 J mol -1 or 53.598 kJ mol -1 or 53.6 kJ mol -1
solution_html:
log {tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2 \cdot 303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex}
log {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_1}=\frac{E a}{2 \cdot 303 \times 8 \cdot 314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{310}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{0 \cdot 3010 \times 19 \cdot 147 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \times 310}{10}{/tex}
Activation energy=Ea = 53598.2 J mol-1 or 53.598 kJ mol-1 or 53.6 kJ mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The following data were obtained for the reaction:
2NO2(g) + F2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2NO2F(g)

Experiment No. [NO2]/M [F2]/M Initial rate M min-1
1 0.2 0.05 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
2 0.4 0.05 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
3 0.8 0.10 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2

Determine the overall order of reaction.
question_text: The following data were obtained for the reaction: 2NO 2 (g) + F 2 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2NO 2 F(g) Experiment No. [NO 2 ]/M [F 2 ]/M Initial rate M min -1 1 0.2 0.05 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 2 0.4 0.05 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 3 0.8 0.10 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 Determine the overall order of reaction.
explanation: Rate = k [NO 2 ] p [F 2 ] q 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k [0.2] p [0.05] q 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = k [0.4] p [0.05] q 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = k [0.8] p [0.1] q From equation 1 and 2 p = 1 From equation 2 and 3 q = 1 Overall order = 2
solution: Rate = k [NO 2 ] p [F 2 ] q 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = k [0.2] p [0.05] q 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = k [0.4] p [0.05] q 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = k [0.8] p [0.1] q From equation 1 and 2 p = 1 From equation 2 and 3 q = 1 Overall order = 2
solution_html:
Rate = k [NO2]p [F2]q

  1. 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k [0.2]p [0.05]q
  2. 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = k [0.4]p [0.05]q
  3. 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = k [0.8]p [0.1]q

From equation 1 and 2
p = 1
From equation 2 and 3
q = 1
Overall order = 2

question_text: For the reaction 2N 2 O 5(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 4NO 2(g) + O 2(g) at 318 K calculate the rate of reaction if rate of disappearance of N 2 O 5(g) is 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m s -1 . For a first order reaction derive the relationship t 99% = 2t 90%
explanation: Rate of reaction = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M s -1 or 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M s -1 First order reaction t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{R}]_0}{[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex} t 99% = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{1}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{k}{/tex} log 100 t 90% = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{10}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{k}{/tex} log 10 t 99% /t 90% = {tex}\frac{\log 100}{\log 10}{/tex} = 2
solution: Rate of reaction = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M s -1 or 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M s -1 First order reaction t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{R}]_0}{[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex} t 99% = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{1}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{k}{/tex} log 100 t 90% = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{10}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{k}{/tex} log 10 t 99% /t 90% = {tex}\frac{\log 100}{\log 10}{/tex} = 2
solution_html:
1. Rate of reaction = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M s-1or 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M s-1
  2. First order reaction t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{R}]_0}{[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex}
    t99% = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{1}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{k}{/tex} log 100
    t90% = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{10}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{k}{/tex} log 10
    t99%/t90% = {tex}\frac{\log 100}{\log 10}{/tex} = 2

question_text: Define rate of reaction. Write two factors that affect the rate of reaction.
explanation: Rate of chemical reaction is equal to change in concentration of reactant and product in unit time. Rate of reaction = {tex}\frac{\text{Change in concentration of reactant and product}}{\text{ Time interval}}{/tex} Two factors: Concentration of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the concentration of reactants. Temperature of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the temperature. For 410 o C rise in temperature rate of reaction generally becomes double.
solution: Rate of chemical reaction is equal to change in concentration of reactant and product in unit time. Rate of reaction = {tex}\frac{\text{Change in concentration of reactant and product}}{\text{ Time interval}}{/tex} Two factors: Concentration of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the concentration of reactants. Temperature of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the temperature. For 410 o C rise in temperature rate of reaction generally becomes double.
solution_html:
Rate of chemical reaction is equal to change in concentration of reactant and product in unit time.
Rate of reaction = {tex}\frac{\text{Change in concentration of reactant and product}}{\text{ Time interval}}{/tex}
Two factors:

  1. Concentration of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the concentration of reactants.
  2. Temperature of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the temperature. For 410o C rise in temperature rate of reaction generally becomes double.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The rate of reaction is concerned with decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of products per unit time. It can be expressed as instantaneous rate at a particular instant of time and average rate over a large interval of time. A number of factors such as temperature, concentration of reactants, catalyst affect the rate of reaction. Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction is given by rate law:
Rate = k[A]x [B]y
x and y indicate how sensitive the rate is to the change in concentration of A and B. Sum of x + y gives the overall order of a reaction.
When a sequence of elementary reactions gives us the products, the reactions are called complex reactions. Molecularity and order of an elementary reaction are same. Zero order reactions are relatively uncommon but they occur under special conditions. All natural and artificial radioactive decay of unstable nuclei take place by first order kinetics.


What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction?
question_text: The rate of reaction is concerned with decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of products per unit time. It can be expressed as instantaneous rate at a particular instant of time and average rate over a large interval of time. Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction is given by rate law: Rate = k[A] x [B] y x and y indicate how sensitive the rate is to the change in concentration of A and B. Sum of x + y gives the overall order of a reaction. When a sequence of elementary reactions gives us the products, the reactions are called complex reactions. Molecularity and order of an elementary reaction are same. Zero order reactions are relatively uncommon but they occur under special conditions. All natural and artificial radioactive decay of unstable nuclei take place by first order kinetics. What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction?
explanation: The rate constant (k) for a reaction increases with an increase in temperature and becomes almost double with every 10 o rises in temperature. This effect is expressed by Arrhenius equation: k = {tex}\frac{A e^{-E a}}{R T}{/tex}
solution: The rate constant (k) for a reaction increases with an increase in temperature and becomes almost double with every 10 o rises in temperature. This effect is expressed by Arrhenius equation: k = {tex}\frac{A e^{-E a}}{R T}{/tex}
solution_html:
The rate constant (k) for a reaction increases with an increase in temperature and becomes almost double with every 10o rises in temperature. This effect is expressed by Arrhenius equation:
k = {tex}\frac{A e^{-E a}}{R T}{/tex}

How order and molecularity are different for complex reactions?
question_text: The rate of reaction is concerned with decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of products per unit time. It can be expressed as instantaneous rate at a particular instant of time and average rate over a large interval of time. A number of factors such as temperature, concentration of reactants, catalyst affect the rate of reaction. Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction is given by rate law: Rate = k[A] x [B] y x and y indicate how sensitive the rate is to the change in concentration of A and B. Sum of x + y gives the overall order of a reaction. When a sequence of elementary reactions gives us the products, the reactions are called complex reactions. Molecularity and order of an elementary reaction are same. Zero order reactions are relatively uncommon but they occur under special conditions. All natural and artificial radioactive decay of unstable nuclei take place by first order kinetics. How order and molecularity are different for complex reactions?
explanation: Order of reaction is defined as the sum of the powers of the molar concentration of the reaction species in the rate equation of the reaction. It is applicable to both elementary and complex reactions. Molecularity of a reaction is defined as the total number of reacting species participating in an elementary reaction. It has no significance for complex reactions as applicable only to elementary reactions.
solution: Order of reaction is defined as the sum of the powers of the molar concentration of the reaction species in the rate equation of the reaction. It is applicable to both elementary and complex reactions. Molecularity of a reaction is defined as the total number of reacting species participating in an elementary reaction. It has no significance for complex reactions as applicable only to elementary reactions.
solution_html:
Order of reaction is defined as the sum of the powers of the molar concentration of the reaction species in the rate equation of the reaction. It is applicable to both elementary and complex reactions.
Molecularity of a reaction is defined as the total number of reacting species participating in an elementary reaction. It has no significance for complex reactions as applicable only to elementary reactions.

explanation: k = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 s -1 t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_0}{R}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \log \frac{6}{2}{/tex} t = 1151.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4771 = 549.3 s

= 549.3 s

[log 4 = 0.6021 log 3 = 0.4771 log 2 = 0.3010 log 10 = 1]
question_text: The rate of reaction is concerned with decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of products per unit time. It can be expressed as instantaneous rate at a particular instant of time and average rate over a large interval of time. A number of factors such as temperature, concentration of reactants, catalyst affect the rate of reaction. Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction is given by rate law: Rate = k[A] x [B] y x and y indicate how sensitive the rate is to the change in concentration of A and B. Sum of x + y gives the overall order of a reaction. When a sequence of elementary reactions gives us the products, the reactions are called complex reactions. Molecularity and order of an elementary reaction are same. Zero order reactions are relatively uncommon but they occur under special conditions. All natural and artificial radioactive decay of unstable nuclei take place by first order kinetics. The half life for radioactive decay of 14 C is 6930 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 75% of the 14 C found in a living tree. Find the age of the sample. [log 4 = 0.6021 log 3 = 0.4771 log 2 = 0.3010 log 10 = 1]
explanation: According to first-order reaction, Half-life (t 1/2 ) = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} t 1/2 = 6980 years k = 0.693/6980 However, the time taken can be calculated using the first-order rate of reaction when wood contains only 75% of C 14 . Initial concentration of C 14 , [R] 0 = 100 Amount at time t s, [R] = 75 t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{6980}} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} t = 2898 years Thus, the age of the sample is approximately 2898 years.
solution: According to first-order reaction, Half-life (t 1/2 ) = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} t 1/2 = 6980 years k = 0.693/6980 However, the time taken can be calculated using the first-order rate of reaction when wood contains only 75% of C 14 . subject: Chemistry
question_html:
In the given reaction
N2(g) + 3H2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NH3 (g)
the rate of formation of NH3 is 3{tex}\cdot{/tex}6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1.
Calculate the

  1. rate of reaction, and
  2. rate of disappearance of H2 (g).
question_text: In the given reaction N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NH 3 (g) the rate of formation of NH 3 is 3 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 . Calculate the rate of reaction, and rate of disappearance of H 2 (g).
explanation: Rate = {tex}\frac{-\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} Rate = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} mol L -1 s -1 = 1 {tex}\cdot {/tex} 8 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 {tex}\frac{-\Delta\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}3 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} mol L -1 s -1 = 5.4 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1
solution: Rate = {tex}\frac{-\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} Rate = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} mol L -1 s -1 = 1 {tex}\cdot {/tex} 8 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 {tex}\frac{-\Delta\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}3 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} mol L -1 s -1 = 5.4 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1
solution_html:
1. Rate = {tex}\frac{-\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex}{tex}\frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex}
    Rate = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} mol L-1 s-1 = 1{tex}\cdot {/tex}8 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1
  2. {tex}\frac{-\Delta\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)}{\Delta t}{/tex} = {tex}3 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} mol L-1 s-1 = 5.4 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1

question_html: In a reaction 2N2O5 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO2 (g) + O2 (g), the concentration of N2O5 decreases from 0·5 mol L-1 to 0·4 mol L-1 in 10 minutes. Calculate the average rate of this reaction and rate of production of NO2 during this period.
question_text: In a reaction 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO 2 (g) + O 2 (g), the concentration of N 2 O 5 decreases from 0·5 mol L -1 to 0·4 mol L -1 in 10 minutes. Calculate the average rate of this reaction and rate of production of NO 2 during this period.
explanation: Average rate = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right)}{\Delta t} \\{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \frac{(0.4-0.5)}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.1}{20}{/tex} = 0.005 mol L -1 min -1 {tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta t}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.005 = 0.002 mol L -1 min -1
solution: Average rate = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right)}{\Delta t} \\{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \frac{(0.4-0.5)}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.1}{20}{/tex} = 0.005 mol L -1 min -1 {tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta t}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.005 = 0.002 mol L -1 min -1
solution_html:
Average rate = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right)}{\Delta t} \\{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \frac{(0.4-0.5)}{10}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.1}{20}{/tex} = 0.005 mol L-1 min-1
{tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\Delta t}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.005 = 0.002 mol L-1 min-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A reaction is first order in X and second order in Y.

  1. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of Y three times?
  2. How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled?
question_text: A reaction is first order in X and second order in Y. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of Y three times? How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled?
explanation: On increasing the concentration of Y three times, rate becomes 9 times. (d[Y]) 2 = 3 2 = 9 When the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled, rate becomes 8 times. d[X](d[Y]) 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 2 = 8
solution: On increasing the concentration of Y three times, rate becomes 9 times. (d[Y]) 2 = 3 2 = 9 When the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled, rate becomes 8 times. d[X](d[Y]) 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 2 = 8
solution_html:
1. On increasing the concentration of Y three times, rate becomes 9 times.
    (d[Y])2 = 32 = 9
  2. When the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled, rate becomes 8 times.
    d[X](d[Y])2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 = 8

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The rate law for a chemical reaction relates the reaction rate with the concentrations or partial pressures of the reactants. For a general reaction aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C with no intermediate steps in its reaction mechanism, meaning that it is an elementary reaction, the rate law is given by r = k[A]x [B]y, where [A] and [B] express the concentrations of A and B in moles per litre. Exponents x and y vary for each reaction and are determined experimentally. The value of k varies with conditions that affect reaction rate, such as temperature, pressure, surface area, etc. The sum of these exponents is known as overall reaction order. A zero order reaction has a constant rate that is independent of the concentration of the reactants. A first order reaction depends on the concentration of only one reactant. A reaction is said to be second order when the overall order is two. Once we have determined the order of the reaction, we can go back and plug in one set of our initial values and solve for k.
