solution: k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{77.78}{/tex} = .008909 min -1 t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{.008909}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{100-30}{/tex} t = 258.5026 (log10 - log7) = 258.5026 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.155 = 40.06 min or 40.02 min
solution_html:
k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{77.78}{/tex} = .008909 min-1
t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{.008909}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{100}{100-30}{/tex}
t = 258.5026 (log10 - log7)
= 258.5026 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.155
= 40.06 min or 40.02 min

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The rate law for a chemical reaction relates the reaction rate with the concentrations or partial pressures of the reactants. For a general reaction aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C with no intermediate steps in its reaction mechanism, meaning that it is an elementary reaction, the rate law is given by r = k[A]x [B]y, where [A] and [B] express the concentrations of A and B in moles per litre. Exponents x and y vary for each reaction and are determined experimentally. The value of k varies with conditions that affect reaction rate, such as temperature, pressure, surface area, etc. The sum of these exponents is known as overall reaction order. A zero order reaction has a constant rate that is independent of the concentration of the reactants. A first order reaction depends on the concentration of only one reactant. A reaction is said to be second order when the overall order is two. Once we have determined the order of the reaction, we can go back and plug in one set of our initial values and solve for k.


A first order reaction has a rate constant 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 per sec. How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 3 g? (log 3 = 0·4771; log 5 = 0·6990)
question_text: The rate law for a chemical reaction relates the reaction rate with the concentrations or partial pressures of the reactants. For a general reaction aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C with no intermediate steps in its reaction mechanism, meaning that it is an elementary reaction, the rate law is given by r = k[A] x [B] y , where [A] and [B] express the concentrations of A and B in moles per litre. Exponents x and y vary for each reaction and are determined experimentally. The value of k varies with conditions that affect reaction rate, such as temperature, pressure, surface area, etc. The sum of these exponents is known as overall reaction order. A zero order reaction has a constant rate that is independent of the concentration of the reactants. A first order reaction depends on the concentration of only one reactant. A reaction is said to be second order when the overall order is two. Once we have determined the order of the reaction, we can go back and plug in one set of our initial values and solve for k. A first order reaction has a rate constant 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 per sec. How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 3 g? (log 3 = 0·4771; log 5 = 0·6990)
explanation: k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} t = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 [0.699 - 0.4771] t = 511 s.
solution: k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} t = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 [0.699 - 0.4771] t = 511 s.
solution_html:
k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex}
t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
t = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex}103 [0.699 - 0.4771]
t = 511 s.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The following reaction was carried out in water:
Br2 + 2I- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Br- + I2
The initial concentration of I- was 0.30 M and the concentration after 10 minutes reduced to 0.28 M. Calculate the rate of disappearance of I- and production of I2.
question_text: The following reaction was carried out in water: Br 2 + 2I - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Br - + I 2 The initial concentration of I - was 0.30 M and the concentration after 10 minutes reduced to 0.28 M. Calculate the rate of disappearance of I - and production of I 2 .
explanation: Rate of disappearing of I - = {tex}\frac{-d[\mathrm{I}^-]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(0.28-0.30)}{10-0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.02}{10}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M min -1 Rate of production of I 2 = {tex}\frac{d[I 2]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-d[I^-]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = 10 -3 M min -1
solution: Rate of disappearing of I - = {tex}\frac{-d[\mathrm{I}^-]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(0.28-0.30)}{10-0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.02}{10}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M min -1 Rate of production of I 2 = {tex}\frac{d[I 2]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-d[I^-]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = 10 -3 M min -1
solution_html:
Rate of disappearing of I- = {tex}\frac{-d[\mathrm{I}^-]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(0.28-0.30)}{10-0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.02}{10}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex}10-3 M min-1
Rate of production of I2 = {tex}\frac{d[I 2]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-d[I^-]}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 10-3 M min-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Following data were obtained for the given reaction:
X + Y {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Product

Exp. [X] / M [Y] / M Initial Rate M min-1
1 0.1 M 0.2 M 0.05
2 0.2 M 0.2 M 0.10
3 0.1 M 0.1 M 0.05
  1. Find the order of reaction with respect to X and Y.
  2. Write the rate law expression.
  3. Find the rate constant.
question_text: Following data were obtained for the given reaction: X + Y {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Product Exp. [X] / M [Y] / M Initial Rate M min -1 1 0.1 M 0.2 M 0.05 2 0.2 M 0.2 M 0.10 3 0.1 M 0.1 M 0.05 Find the order of reaction with respect to X and Y. Write the rate law expression. Find the rate constant.
explanation: Rate = k [X] a [Y] b 0.05 = k [0.1] a [0.2] b ...(i) 0.10 = k [0.2] a [0.2] b ...(ii) 0.05 = k [0.1] a [0.1] b ...(iii) On solving a= 1 and b = 0. Order w.r.t X = 1 and Order w.r.t Y = 0 Rate = k [X] 1 [Y] 0 Rate = k [X] or 0.05 = k [0.1] K = {tex}\frac{0.05}{0.1}{/tex} = 0.5 min -1
solution: Rate = k [X] a [Y] b 0.05 = k [0.1] a [0.2] b ...(i) 0.10 = k [0.2] a [0.2] b ...(ii) 0.05 = k [0.1] a [0.1] b ...(iii) On solving a= 1 and b = 0. Order w.r.t X = 1 and Order w.r.t Y = 0 Rate = k [X] 1 [Y] 0 Rate = k [X] or 0.05 = k [0.1] K = {tex}\frac{0.05}{0.1}{/tex} = 0.5 min -1
solution_html:
1. Rate = k [X]a [Y]b
    0.05 = k [0.1]a [0.2]b ...(i)
    0.10 = k [0.2]a [0.2]b ...(ii)
    0.05 = k [0.1]a [0.1]b ...(iii)
    On solving a= 1 and b = 0.
    Order w.r.t X = 1 and Order w.r.t Y = 0
  2. Rate = k [X]1 [Y]0
  3. Rate = k [X] or 0.05 = k [0.1]
    K = {tex}\frac{0.05}{0.1}{/tex} = 0.5 min-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For a reaction the rate law expression is represented as follows:
Rate = {tex}k[ A ][ B ]^\frac {1 }{ 2}{/tex}

  1. Interpret whether the reaction is elementary or complex. Give reason to support your answer.
  2. Write the unit of rate constant for this reaction if concentration of A and B is expressed in moles/L.
question_text: For a reaction the rate law expression is represented as follows: Rate = {tex}k[ A ][ B ]^\frac {1 }{ 2}{/tex} Interpret whether the reaction is elementary or complex. Give reason to support your answer. Write the unit of rate constant for this reaction if concentration of A and B is expressed in moles/L.
explanation: This is a complex reaction. Order of reaction is 1.5 . Molecularity cannot be 1.5, it has no meaning for this reaction. The reaction occurs in steps, so it is a complex reaction. Rate = {tex}k[ A ][ B ]^{[\frac{1 }{ 2}]}{/tex} Unit of Rate constant (k) {tex}=\frac{\text { Unit of rate }}{\text { Unit of }[ A ] \times \text { Unit of }[ B ]^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{ molL ^{-1} s ^{-1}}{\left( mol L ^{-1}\right)\left( mol L ^{-1}\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}= mol ^\frac{-1 }{ 2} L ^\frac {1 }{2} s ^1{/tex}
solution: This is a complex reaction. Order of reaction is 1.5 . Molecularity cannot be 1.5, it has no meaning for this reaction. The reaction occurs in steps, so it is a complex reaction. Rate = {tex}k[ A ][ B ]^{[\frac{1 }{ 2}]}{/tex} Unit of Rate constant (k) {tex}=\frac{\text { Unit of rate }}{\text { Unit of }[ A ] \times \text { Unit of }[ B ]^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{ molL ^{-1} s ^{-1}}{\left( mol L ^{-1}\right)\left( mol L ^{-1}\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}= mol ^\frac{-1 }{ 2} L ^\frac {1 }{2} s ^1{/tex}
solution_html:
1. This is a complex reaction.
    Order of reaction is 1.5 . Molecularity cannot be 1.5, it has no meaning for this reaction. The reaction occurs in steps, so it is a complex reaction.
  2. Rate = {tex}k[ A ][ B ]^{[\frac{1 }{ 2}]}{/tex}
    Unit of Rate constant (k)
    {tex}=\frac{\text { Unit of rate }}{\text { Unit of }[ A ] \times \text { Unit of }[ B ]^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{ molL ^{-1} s ^{-1}}{\left( mol L ^{-1}\right)\left( mol L ^{-1}\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}= mol ^\frac{-1 }{ 2} L ^\frac {1 }{2} s ^1{/tex}

question_text: Calculate the overall order of the reaction whose rate law expression was predicted as: Rate = k[NO] 3/2 [O 2 ] 1/2
explanation: Rate = k [NO] x [O 2 ] y order of a reaction = x + y So order = {tex}\frac {1} {2} + \frac {3} {2}{/tex} = 2, i.e., second order of reaction.
solution: Rate = k [NO] x [O 2 ] y order of a reaction = x + y So order = {tex}\frac {1} {2} + \frac {3} {2}{/tex} = 2, i.e., second order of reaction.

order of a reaction = x + y
So order = {tex}\frac {1} {2} + \frac {3} {2}{/tex} = 2, i.e., second order of reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A first order reaction is 40% complete in 80 minutes. Calculate the value of rate constant (k). In what time will the reaction be 90% completed? 
[Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0·6021, log 5 = 0.6771, log 6 = 0.7782]
question_text: A first order reaction is 40% complete in 80 minutes. Calculate the value of rate constant (k). In what time will the reaction be 90% completed? [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0·6021, log 5 = 0.6771, log 6 = 0.7782]
explanation: For a first order reaction: k t = 2.303 log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex} k t = 2.303 log {tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} Here, given that x = {tex}\frac{40}{100} a{/tex} t = 80 minutes (given) k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{a}{a-0.4 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{a}{0.6 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{10}{6}{/tex} The value of rate constant = 0.00638 min -1 When the reaction is 90% complete x = {tex}\frac{90}{100} a{/tex} = 0.9 a, t = ? k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-0.9 a}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.00638} \log 10{/tex} = 360.971 min
solution: For a first order reaction: k t = 2.303 log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex} k t = 2.303 log {tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} Here, given that x = {tex}\frac{40}{100} a{/tex} t = 80 minutes (given) k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{a}{a-0.4 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{a}{0.6 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{10}{6}{/tex} The value of rate constant = 0.00638 min -1 When the reaction is 90% complete x = {tex}\frac{90}{100} a{/tex} = 0.9 a, t = ? k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-0.9 a}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.00638} \log 10{/tex} = 360.971 min
solution_html:
For a first order reaction:
kt = 2.303 log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex} 
kt = 2.303 log {tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} 
Here, given that x = {tex}\frac{40}{100} a{/tex} 
t = 80 minutes (given)
k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{a}{a-0.4 a}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{a}{0.6 a}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{80} \log \frac{10}{6}{/tex} 
The value of rate constant = 0.00638 min-1 
When the reaction is 90% complete
x = {tex}\frac{90}{100} a{/tex} = 0.9 a, t = ?
k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-0.9 a}{/tex} 
t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.00638} \log 10{/tex} 
= 360.971 min

question_text: A reaction is first order w.r.t. reactant A as well as w.r.t. reactant B. Give the rate law. Also give one point of difference between average rate and instantaneous rate.
explanation: Since the reaction is of first-order wrt A and second-order wrt B, then the rate law can be given as, (Rate) = k [A] [B] 2 Average rate (R av ) = Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in the chemical reaction in a specified time interval. Instantaneous rate(R av ) = Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product in the chemical reaction at a particular moment of time.
solution: Since the reaction is of first-order wrt A and second-order wrt B, then the rate law can be given as, (Rate) = k [A] [B] 2 Average rate (R av ) = Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in the chemical reaction in a specified time interval. Instantaneous rate(R av ) = Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product in the chemical reaction at a particular moment of time.
solution_html:
Since the reaction is of first-order wrt A and second-order wrt B, then the rate law can be given as,
(Rate) = k [A] [B]2 
Average rate (Rav) = Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in the chemical reaction in a specified time interval.
Instantaneous rate(Rav) = Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product in the chemical reaction at a particular moment of time.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The rate constant for a first order reaction is 60 s-1. How much time will it take to reduce the initial concentration of the reactant to its {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex}th value?
  2. Write two factors that affect the rate of a chemical reaction.
  3. Write two conditions for the collisions to be effective collisions.
question_text: The rate constant for a first order reaction is 60 s -1 . How much time will it take to reduce the initial concentration of the reactant to its {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} th value? Write two factors that affect the rate of a chemical reaction. Write two conditions for the collisions to be effective collisions.
explanation: Given that the rate constant for first-order reaction is 60s -1 . It is known that, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{16}}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log 16{/tex} = 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s (approximately) Hence, the required time is 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s Factors Affecting the Rate of Reaction Concentration of reactants Temperature The two conditions that are necessary for effective collisions are: The molecules should collide with sufficient kinetic energy also called the threshold energy. The molecules should have proper orientation.
solution: Given that the rate constant for first-order reaction is 60s -1 . It is known that, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{16}}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log 16{/tex} = 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s (approximately) Hence, the required time is 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s Factors Affecting the Rate of Reaction Concentration of reactants Temperature The two conditions that are necessary for effective collisions are: The molecules should collide with sufficient kinetic energy also called the threshold energy. The molecules should have proper orientation.
solution_html:
1. Given  that the rate constant for first-order reaction is 60s-1.
    It is known that,
    {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{16}}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log 16{/tex} 
    = 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 s (approximately)
    Hence, the required time is 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 s
  2. Factors Affecting the Rate of Reaction
    1. Concentration of reactants
    2. Temperature
  3. The two conditions that are necessary for effective collisions are:
    1. The molecules should collide with sufficient kinetic energy also called the threshold energy.
    2. The molecules should have proper orientation.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. A first-order reaction is 50% complete in 30 minutes at 300 K and in 10 minutes at 320 K. Calculate activation energy (Ea) for the reaction. (R = 8.314 J K-1 mol-1)
  2. Write the two conditions for collisions to be effective collisions.
  3. How order of reaction and molecularity differ towards a complex reaction?
    [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021, log 5 = 0.6991]
question_text: A first-order reaction is 50% complete in 30 minutes at 300 K and in 10 minutes at 320 K. Calculate activation energy (E a ) for the reaction. (R = 8.314 J K -1 mol -1 ) Write the two conditions for collisions to be effective collisions. How order of reaction and molecularity differ towards a complex reaction? [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021, log 5 = 0.6991]
explanation: At 300 K, t 1/2 = 30 min t 1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} or k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}{/tex} k 1 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{30}{/tex} = 0.0231 min -1 At 320 K, t 1/2 = 10 min t 1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} or k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}{/tex} k 2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{10}{/tex} = 0.0693 min -1 According to Arrhenius equation: {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_{1}}-\frac{1}{T_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{0.0693}{0.0231}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{0.0693}{0.0231}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{320-300}{300 \times 320}\right]{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 ] log 3 = {tex}\frac{E_{a} \times 20}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{/tex} E a = {tex}\frac{\log 3 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} log 3 = 0.4771] = {tex}\frac{0.4771 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} = 43848.5 J mol -1 = 43.85 kJ mol -1 Two conditions for collisions to be effective collision are: The reactant molecules must have attained sufficient energy, known as activation energy to break chemical bonds The reactant molecules must have the proper orientation. The number of ions or molecules that take place in the rate-determining step is called as molecularity of a reaction. The sum of the coefficients of the reacting species is the order of a reaction. For complex reactions, molecularity has no significance while the order of a reaction is applicable.
solution: At 300 K, t 1/2 = 30 min t 1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} or k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}{/tex} k 1 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{30}{/tex} = 0.0231 min -1 At 320 K, t 1/2 = 10 min t 1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} or k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}{/tex} k 2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{10}{/tex} = 0.0693 min -1 According to Arrhenius equation: {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_{1}}-\frac{1}{T_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{0.0693}{0.0231}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{0.0693}{0.0231}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{320-300}{300 \times 320}\right]{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 ] log 3 = {tex}\frac{E_{a} \times 20}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{/tex} E a = {tex}\frac{\log 3 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} log 3 = 0.4771] = {tex}\frac{0.4771 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} = 43848.5 J mol -1 = 43.85 kJ mol -1 Two conditions for collisions to be effective collision are: The reactant molecules must have attained sufficient energy, known as activation energy to break chemical bonds The reactant molecules must have the proper orientation. The number of ions or molecules that take place in the rate-determining step is called as molecularity of a reaction. The sum of the coefficients of the reacting species is the order of a reaction. For complex reactions, molecularity has no significance while the order of a reaction is applicable.
solution_html:
1. At 300 K, t1/2 = 30 min
    t1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} or k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}{/tex} 
    k1 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{30}{/tex} = 0.0231 min-1 
    At 320 K, t1/2 = 10 min
    t1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} or k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}{/tex} 
    k2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{10}{/tex} = 0.0693 min-1 
    According to Arrhenius equation:
    {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_{1}}-\frac{1}{T_{2}}\right]{/tex} 
    {tex}\log \frac{0.0693}{0.0231}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex} 
    {tex}\log \frac{0.0693}{0.0231}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{320-300}{300 \times 320}\right]{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1]
    log 3 = {tex}\frac{E_{a} \times 20}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{/tex} 
    Ea = {tex}\frac{\log 3 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} log 3 = 0.4771]
    = {tex}\frac{0.4771 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 320}{20}{/tex} 
    = 43848.5 J mol-1 = 43.85 kJ mol-1 
  2. Two conditions for collisions to be effective collision are:
    1. The reactant molecules must have attained sufficient energy, known as activation energy to break chemical bonds
    2. The reactant molecules must have the proper orientation.
  3. The number of ions or molecules that take place in the rate-determining step is called as molecularity of a reaction. The sum of the coefficients of the reacting species is the order of a reaction.
    For complex reactions, molecularity has no significance while the order of a reaction is applicable.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. A first order reaction is 25% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the value of rate constant. In what time will the reaction be 80% completed?
  2. Define order of reaction. Write the condition under which a bimolecular reaction follows first order kinetics.
question_text: A first order reaction is 25% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the value of rate constant. In what time will the reaction be 80% completed? Define order of reaction. Write the condition under which a bimolecular reaction follows first order kinetics.
explanation: Given, first order reaction is 25% completed in 40mint.so the time required for 80% completion of reaction is t= {tex}\frac{2303}{k} \log \frac{[A]_{0}}{[A]}{/tex} In 40 min = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} ...(i) t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{20}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing equation (i) by (ii) {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{75} / \frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{4}{3}}{2.303 \log 5}{/tex} {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{0.6021 / 4.771}{0.6991}{/tex} {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{0.1250}{0.6991}{/tex} time = {tex}\frac{0.6691 \times 40}{0.1250}{/tex} = 223.712 min, therefore, time required for 80%completion of reaction is 223.712min Value of rate constant k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{100-0.25}=\frac{2.303}{40} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40}(\log 4-\log 3){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40}(0.6021-0.4771){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40} \times 0.125{/tex} = 0.007196 = 7.196 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 min -1 Order of reaction: The sum of the coefficients of the reacting species that are involved in the rate equation for the reaction, is called the order of the reaction. The condition under which a bimolecular reaction follows first-order kinetics is when one of the reactants is taken in large excess that its concentration changes hardly. Such a reactant will not contribute to order. Thus, a bimolecular reaction will be of the first order.
solution: Given, first order reaction is 25% completed in 40mint.so the time required for 80% completion of reaction is t= {tex}\frac{2303}{k} \log \frac{[A]_{0}}{[A]}{/tex} In 40 min = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} ...(i) t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{20}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing equation (i) by (ii) {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{75} / \frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{4}{3}}{2.303 \log 5}{/tex} {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{0.6021 / 4.771}{0.6991}{/tex} {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{0.1250}{0.6991}{/tex} time = {tex}\frac{0.6691 \times 40}{0.1250}{/tex} = 223.712 min, therefore, time required for 80%completion of reaction is 223.712min Value of rate constant k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{100-0.25}=\frac{2.303}{40} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40}(\log 4-\log 3){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40}(0.6021-0.4771){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40} \times 0.125{/tex} = 0.007196 = 7.196 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 min -1 Order of reaction: The sum of the coefficients of the reacting species that are involved in the rate equation for the reaction, is called the order of the reaction. The condition under which a bimolecular reaction follows first-order kinetics is when one of the reactants is taken in large excess that its concentration changes hardly. Such a reactant will not contribute to order. Thus, a bimolecular reaction will be of the first order.
solution_html:
1. Given, first order reaction is 25% completed in 40mint.so the time required for 80% completion of reaction is t= {tex}\frac{2303}{k} \log \frac{[A]_{0}}{[A]}{/tex} 
    In 40 min = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} ...(i)
    t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{20}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Dividing equation (i) by (ii)
    {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{75} / \frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{20}{/tex} 
    {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{4}{3}}{2.303 \log 5}{/tex} 
    {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{0.6021 / 4.771}{0.6991}{/tex} 
    {tex}\frac{40}{t}=\frac{0.1250}{0.6991}{/tex} 
    time  = {tex}\frac{0.6691 \times 40}{0.1250}{/tex} = 223.712 min, therefore, time required for 80%completion of reaction is 223.712min
    Value of rate constant k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{100-0.25}=\frac{2.303}{40} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40}(\log 4-\log 3){/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40}(0.6021-0.4771){/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{2.303}{40} \times 0.125{/tex} = 0.007196 = 7.196 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 min-1 

  2. Order of reaction: The sum of the coefficients of the reacting species that are involved in the rate equation for the reaction, is called the order of the reaction.
    The condition under which a bimolecular reaction follows first-order kinetics is when one of the reactants is taken in large excess that its concentration changes hardly. Such a reactant will not contribute to order. Thus, a bimolecular reaction will be of the first order.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
In the given reaction
A + 3B {tex}\to{/tex} 2C,
the rate of formation of C is 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1. Calculate the

  1. rate of reaction, and
  2. rate of disappearance of B.
question_text: In the given reaction A + 3B {tex}\to{/tex} 2C, the rate of formation of C is 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 . Calculate the rate of reaction, and rate of disappearance of B.
explanation: In the reaction A + 3B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2C If, rate of formation of {tex}C\left(\frac{\Delta c}{\Delta t}\right)=2.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} mol L -1 s -1 then rate of reaction {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta c}{\Delta t}\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} {tex} =1.25 \times 10^{-4} {molL}^{-} {s}{/tex} If rate of reaction is 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 then, {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}=1.25 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} Rate of disappearance of {tex}B\left(\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}3.75 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} mol L -1 s -1
solution: In the reaction A + 3B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2C If, rate of formation of {tex}C\left(\frac{\Delta c}{\Delta t}\right)=2.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} mol L -1 s -1 then rate of reaction {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta c}{\Delta t}\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} {tex} =1.25 \times 10^{-4} {molL}^{-} {s}{/tex} If rate of reaction is 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 then, {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}=1.25 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} Rate of disappearance of {tex}B\left(\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}3.75 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} mol L -1 s -1
solution_html:
In the reaction
A + 3B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2C
If, rate of formation of {tex}C\left(\frac{\Delta c}{\Delta t}\right)=2.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} mol L-1 s-1
then

  1. rate of reaction {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta c}{\Delta t}\right){/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex}
    {tex} =1.25 \times 10^{-4} {molL}^{-} {s}{/tex}
  2. If rate of reaction is 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol  L-1 s-1
    then,
    {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}=1.25 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
    Rate of disappearance of {tex}B\left(\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=3 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
    {tex}3.75 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} mol L-1 s-1

question_text: How will the rate of the reaction be affected when surface area of the reactant is increased, temperature of the reaction is decreased, and catalyst is added in a reversible reaction?
explanation: The rate of reaction will increase if the surface area of the reactant is increased. will decrease if temperature of the reaction is decreased, and will increase until the reaction attains equilibrium if a catalyst is added in a reversible reaction.
solution: The rate of reaction will increase if the surface area of the reactant is increased. will decrease if temperature of the reaction is decreased, and will increase until the reaction attains equilibrium if a catalyst is added in a reversible reaction.

The rate of reaction

  1. will increase if the surface area of the reactant is increased.
  2. will decrease if temperature of the reaction is decreased, and
  3. will increase until the reaction attains equilibrium if a catalyst is added in a reversible reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Define order of reaction. How does order of a reaction differ from molecularity for a complex reaction?
  2. A first order reaction is 50% complete in 25 minutes. Calculate the time for 80% completion of the reaction.
question_text: Define order of reaction. How does order of a reaction differ from molecularity for a complex reaction? A first order reaction is 50% complete in 25 minutes. Calculate the time for 80% completion of the reaction.
explanation: Order of a reaction: Sum of the powers to which the concentration terms are raised in the rate law equation. Order of reaction is applicable for complex reaction but molecularity has no meaning for the complex reaction. We know that, {tex}log(\frac{N}{N_\circ})=\frac{t}{t_\frac{1}{2}}\times log(\frac{1}{2}){/tex} N = amount left after decay = 100 - 80 = 20 N o = initial amount = 100 t = time for 80% completion reaction​ half life = 25 minutes Substitute all values in formula, we get {tex}log(\frac{20}{100})=\frac{t}{25}\times log(\frac{1}{2})\\t=58 \ min.{/tex}
solution: Order of a reaction: Sum of the powers to which the concentration terms are raised in the rate law equation. Order of reaction is applicable for complex reaction but molecularity has no meaning for the complex reaction. We know that, {tex}log(\frac{N}{N_\circ})=\frac{t}{t_\frac{1}{2}}\times log(\frac{1}{2}){/tex} N = amount left after decay = 100 - 80 = 20 N o = initial amount = 100 t = time for 80% completion reaction​ half life = 25 minutes Substitute all values in formula, we get {tex}log(\frac{20}{100})=\frac{t}{25}\times log(\frac{1}{2})\\t=58 \ min.{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Order of a reaction: Sum of the powers to which the concentration terms are raised in the rate law equation.
    Order of reaction is applicable for complex reaction but molecularity has no meaning for the complex reaction.
  2. We know that, 
    {tex}log(\frac{N}{N_\circ})=\frac{t}{t_\frac{1}{2}}\times log(\frac{1}{2}){/tex}
    N = amount left after decay = 100 - 80 = 20
    No= initial amount = 100
    t = time for 80% completion reaction​ 
    half life = 25 minutes
    Substitute all values in formula, we get
    {tex}log(\frac{20}{100})=\frac{t}{25}\times log(\frac{1}{2})\\t=58 \ min.{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For a reaction,
{tex}2 H _2 O _2 \xrightarrow[\text { alkaline medium }]{ I ^{-}} 2 H _2 O + O _2{/tex}
The proposed mechanism is given below:
(1) H2O2 + I- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O + IO-(slow)
(2) H2O2 + IO- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O + I- + O2(fast)

  1. Write rate law of the reaction.
  2. Write overall order of reaction.
  3. Out of step (1) and (2), which one is rate determining step?
question_text: For a reaction, {tex}2 H _2 O _2 \xrightarrow[\text { alkaline medium }]{ I ^{-}} 2 H _2 O + O _2{/tex} The proposed mechanism is given below: (1) H 2 O 2 + I - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H 2 O + IO - (slow) (2) H 2 O 2 + IO - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H 2 O + I - + O 2 (fast) Write rate law of the reaction. Write overall order of reaction. Out of step (1) and (2), which one is rate determining step?
explanation: Rate law for the reaction Rate = K [H 2 O 2 ] [I - ], where K is the rate constant for the given reaction. overall order of reaction = 1 + 1 = 2 Step (1) is the rate determining step because it is the slowest step (elementary reaction) in the proposed mechanism hence the rate of reaction is determined by this reaction (step).
solution: Rate law for the reaction Rate = K [H 2 O 2 ] [I - ], where K is the rate constant for the given reaction. overall order of reaction = 1 + 1 = 2 Step (1) is the rate determining step because it is the slowest step (elementary reaction) in the proposed mechanism hence the rate of reaction is determined by this reaction (step).
solution_html:
1. Rate law for the reaction
    Rate = K [H2O2] [I-], where K is the rate constant for the given reaction.
  2. overall order of reaction = 1 + 1 = 2
  3. Step (1) is the rate determining step because it is the slowest step (elementary reaction) in the proposed mechanism hence the rate of reaction is determined by this reaction (step).

question_html:
The reaction between A and B is first order with respect to A and zero order with respect to B. Fill in the blanks in the following table:

Experiment A/mol L-1 B/mol L-1 Initial rate /mol L-1 min-1
I 0.1 0.1 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
II - 0.2 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
III 0.4 0.4 -
IV - 0.2 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
question_text: The reaction between A and B is first order with respect to A and zero order with respect to B. Fill in the blanks in the following table: Experiment A/mol L -1 B/mol L -1 Initial rate /mol L -1 min -1 I 0.1 0.1 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 II - 0.2 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 III 0.4 0.4 - IV - 0.2 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2
explanation: The given reaction is of the first order with respect to A and of zero order with respect to B. Therefore, the rate of the reaction is given by, {tex}Rate=k{{[A]}^{1}}{{[B]}^{0}}{/tex} Rate = k [A] From experiment I, we obtain {tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=k(0.1\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}){/tex} {tex}k=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment II, we obtain {tex}4.0\times {{10}^{-2}}\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}\,{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex} , so {tex}[A]=0.2mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment III, we obtain {tex}Rate=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}\times 0.4mol\,{{L}^{-1}} =0.08\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment IV, we obtain {tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2\,{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex} , so {tex}[A]=0.1mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex}
solution: The given reaction is of the first order with respect to A and of zero order with respect to B. Therefore, the rate of the reaction is given by, {tex}Rate=k{{[A]}^{1}}{{[B]}^{0}}{/tex} Rate = k [A] From experiment I, we obtain {tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=k(0.1\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}){/tex} {tex}k=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment II, we obtain {tex}4.0\times {{10}^{-2}}\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}\,{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex} , so {tex}[A]=0.2mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment III, we obtain {tex}Rate=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}\times 0.4mol\,{{L}^{-1}} =0.08\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment IV, we obtain {tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2\,{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex} , so {tex}[A]=0.1mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given reaction is of the first order with respect to A and of zero order with respect to B.
Therefore, the rate of the reaction is given by,
{tex}Rate=k{{[A]}^{1}}{{[B]}^{0}}{/tex}
Rate = k [A]
From experiment I, we obtain
{tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=k(0.1\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}){/tex}
{tex}k=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex}
From experiment II, we obtain
{tex}4.0\times {{10}^{-2}}\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}\,{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex}, so {tex}[A]=0.2mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex}
From experiment III, we obtain
{tex}Rate=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}\times 0.4mol\,{{L}^{-1}} =0.08\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex}
From experiment IV, we obtain
{tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2\,{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex}, so {tex}[A]=0.1mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For the reaction
{tex}2 N _2 O _5( g ) \longrightarrow 4 NO _2( g )+ O _2( g ) \text {, }{/tex}
the rate of formation of NO2 (g) is 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M s-1. Calculate the rate of disappearance of N2O5 (g).
question_text: For the reaction {tex}2 N _2 O _5( g ) \longrightarrow 4 NO _2( g )+ O _2( g ) \text {, }{/tex} the rate of formation of NO 2 (g) is 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M s -1 . Calculate the rate of disappearance of N 2 O 5 (g).
explanation: For the reaction 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4NO 2 (g) + O 2 (g), the rate of formation of NO 2 (g) is 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 Ms -1 . {tex}\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ NO _2\right]=2.8 \times 10^{-3} Ms ^{-1}{/tex} The rate of disappearance of N 2 O 5 (g). {tex}-\frac{1 d }{2 dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1 d }{4 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=2 \times \frac{1 d }{4 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1 d }{2 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1}{2} \times 2.8 \times 10^{-3} Ms ^{-1}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]{/tex} = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 Ms -1
solution: For the reaction 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4NO 2 (g) + O 2 (g), the rate of formation of NO 2 (g) is 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 Ms -1 . {tex}\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ NO _2\right]=2.8 \times 10^{-3} Ms ^{-1}{/tex} The rate of disappearance of N 2 O 5 (g). {tex}-\frac{1 d }{2 dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1 d }{4 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=2 \times \frac{1 d }{4 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1 d }{2 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1}{2} \times 2.8 \times 10^{-3} Ms ^{-1}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]{/tex} = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 Ms -1
solution_html:
For the reaction 2N2O5(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4NO2(g) + O2(g), the rate of formation of NO2(g) is
2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 Ms-1.
{tex}\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ NO _2\right]=2.8 \times 10^{-3} Ms ^{-1}{/tex}
The rate of disappearance of N2O5(g).
{tex}-\frac{1 d }{2 dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1 d }{4 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex}
{tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=2 \times \frac{1 d }{4 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex}
{tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1 d }{2 dt }\left[ NO _2\right]{/tex}
{tex}-\frac{ d }{ dt }\left[ N _2 O _5\right]=\frac{1}{2} \times 2.8 \times 10^{-3} Ms ^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]{/tex} = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 Ms-1

solution: k 2 = {tex}\frac {0.693}{ 20}{/tex} k 1 = {tex}\frac {0.693 }{ 40}{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{k_2}{k_1} =\frac{ E _a}{2.303 R }\left[\frac{1}{ T _1}-\frac{1}{ T _2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac {k_2 }{ k_1}{/tex} = 2 log 2 {tex}=\frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{320-300}{320 \times 300}\right]{/tex} E a = 27663.8 J/mol or 27.66 kJ/mol
solution_html:
k2 = {tex}\frac {0.693}{ 20}{/tex}
k1 = {tex}\frac {0.693 }{ 40}{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{k_2}{k_1} =\frac{ E _a}{2.303 R }\left[\frac{1}{ T _1}-\frac{1}{ T _2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac {k_2 }{ k_1}{/tex} = 2
log 2 {tex}=\frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{320-300}{320 \times 300}\right]{/tex}
Ea = 27663.8 J/mol or 27.66 kJ/mol

What is the effect of adding a catalyst on

  1. Activation energy (Ea) and
  2. Gibbs energy ({tex}\Delta G{/tex}) of a reaction?
question_text: What is the effect of adding a catalyst on Activation energy (E a ) and Gibbs energy ( {tex}\Delta G{/tex} ) of a reaction?
explanation: Catalyst lowers the activation energy and changes the path of the reaction. A catalyst does not alter Gibbs energy {tex}(\Delta G){/tex} of a reaction.
solution: Catalyst lowers the activation energy and changes the path of the reaction. A catalyst does not alter Gibbs energy {tex}(\Delta G){/tex} of a reaction.

question_text: A first order reaction takes 20 minutes for 25% decomposition. Calculate the time when 75% of the reaction will be completed. Given : log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021
explanation: For a fiirst order reaction, {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} where, k = Rate constant a = Initial concentration (a - x) =concentration after time 't', When a first order reaction is 25% completed in 20 min. a = 100, a - x = 100 - 25 = 75, t = 20 min. {tex}\therefore \quad k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}=\frac{2.303}{20} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{20}{/tex} [log 4 - log 3] = 0.0143 min -1 For 75% completion of reaction. a = 100, a - x = 100 - 75 = 25, k = 0.0143 min -1 For 75% completion of reaction. a = 100, a - x = 100 - 75 = 25, k = 0.0143 min -1 {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}=\frac{2303}{0.0143} \log \frac{100}{25}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{0.0143} \log 4{/tex} = 96.968

t/s 0 300 600
[N2O5]/mol L-1 1.6{tex}\times{/tex}10-2 0.8{tex}\times{/tex}10-2 0.4{tex}\times{/tex}10-2

    (Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 4 = 0.6021)
question_text: Following data are obtained for the reaction {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex} t/s 0 300 600 [N 2 O 5 ]/mol L -1 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 Show that it follows first order reaction. Calculate the half-life. (Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 4 = 0.6021)
explanation: When time and rate of a reaction is given then we can use hit and trial method to find out the order of reaction by substituting the values in different integrated equation. Using data, to find out the rate constant. t/s 0 300 600 [N2O5]/mol L-1 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 Using hit and trial method, For first order reaction, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} Here, t = 300s, [R]0 = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 mol L -1 and [R] = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 mol L -1 {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{300} \log \frac{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-2}\right) \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{\left(0.8 \times 10^{-2}\right) \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{300} \log 2=\frac{2303}{300} \times 0.3010{/tex} k = 2.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 s -1 Thus, it is proved that reaction proceed through first order kinetics as the rate constant remains same. Half-life {tex}\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{2.31 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{s}{/tex} t 1/2 = 300

Half-life {tex}\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{2.31 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{s}{/tex} t 1/2 = 300

1. When time and rate of a reaction is given then we can use hit and trial method to find out the order of reaction by substituting the values in different integrated equation.
    Using data, to find out the rate constant.

    Using hit and trial method,
    For first order reaction, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    Here, t = 300s, [R]0 = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-2 mol L-1
    and [R] = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex}10-2 mol L-1
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{300} \log \frac{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-2}\right) \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{\left(0.8 \times 10^{-2}\right) \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{300} \log 2=\frac{2303}{300} \times 0.3010{/tex}
    k = 2.31 {tex}\times{/tex}10-3 s-1
    Thus, it is proved that reaction proceed through first order kinetics as the rate constant remains same.

  2. Half-life {tex}\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Derive integrated rate equation for rate constant of a first order reaction.
question_text: Derive integrated rate equation for rate constant of a first order reaction.
explanation: R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P Rate {tex}=-\frac{d[R]}{d t}=k[R]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d[ R ]}{[ R ]}=-k d t{/tex} Integrating this equation, we get ln [R] = -kt + I ...(i) When t = 0, R = [R] 0 . where, [R] 0 is the initial concentration of the reactant. Therefore, equation (i) can be written as ln [R] 0 = -k {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 1 ln [R] 0 = 1 Substituting the value of I in equation (i) ln [R] = -kt + ln [R] 0 Rearranging this equation {tex}\ln \frac{[ R ]}{[ R ]_0} =-k t{/tex} {tex}k =\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{[ R ]_0}{[ R ]}{/tex} {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_0}{[ R ]}{/tex}
solution: R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P Rate {tex}=-\frac{d[R]}{d t}=k[R]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d[ R ]}{[ R ]}=-k d t{/tex} Integrating this equation, we get ln [R] = -kt + I ...(i) When t = 0, R = [R] 0 . where, [R] 0 is the initial concentration of the reactant. Therefore, equation (i) can be written as ln [R] 0 = -k {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 1 ln [R] 0 = 1 Substituting the value of I in equation (i) ln [R] = -kt + ln [R] 0 Rearranging this equation {tex}\ln \frac{[ R ]}{[ R ]_0} =-k t{/tex} {tex}k =\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{[ R ]_0}{[ R ]}{/tex} {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_0}{[ R ]}{/tex}
solution_html:
R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P
Rate {tex}=-\frac{d[R]}{d t}=k[R]{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d[ R ]}{[ R ]}=-k d t{/tex}
Integrating this equation, we get
ln [R] = -kt + I ...(i)
When t = 0, R = [R]0.
where, [R]0 is the initial concentration of the reactant.
Therefore, equation (i) can be written as
ln [R]0 = -k {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 1
ln [R]0 = 1
Substituting the value of I in equation (i)
ln [R] = -kt + ln [R]0
Rearranging this equation
{tex}\ln \frac{[ R ]}{[ R ]_0} =-k t{/tex}
{tex}k =\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{[ R ]_0}{[ R ]}{/tex}
{tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_0}{[ R ]}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the rate law for a first order reaction. Justify the statement that half life for a first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant.
  2. For a first order reaction, show that the time required for 99% completion of a first order reaction is twice the time required for the completion of 90%.
question_text: Write the rate law for a first order reaction. Justify the statement that half life for a first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. For a first order reaction, show that the time required for 99% completion of a first order reaction is twice the time required for the completion of 90%.
explanation: For a first order reaction {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{[ R ]}{/tex} where [R] o = initial concentration, [R] = conc. after time t When half of the reaction is completed, [R] = {tex}\frac {[R]_o}{2}{/tex} . Representing, the time taken for half of the reaction to be completed, by {tex}t_\frac 12{/tex} , equation becomes: {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t_\frac {1 }{ 2}} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{\frac {[ R ]_{ o }}{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} The above equation shows that half-life of first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. For a first order reaction {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 100{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303 \times 2}{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4.606}{k}{/tex} and {tex}t_{90 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}} =2{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =2 \times t_{90 \%}{/tex}
solution: For a first order reaction {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{[ R ]}{/tex} where [R] o = initial concentration, [R] = conc. after time t When half of the reaction is completed, [R] = {tex}\frac {[R]_o}{2}{/tex} . Representing, the time taken for half of the reaction to be completed, by {tex}t_\frac 12{/tex} , equation becomes: {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t_\frac {1 }{ 2}} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{\frac {[ R ]_{ o }}{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} The above equation shows that half-life of first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. For a first order reaction {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 100{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303 \times 2}{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4.606}{k}{/tex} and {tex}t_{90 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}} =2{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =2 \times t_{90 \%}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. For a first order reaction
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{[ R ]}{/tex}
    where [R]o = initial concentration, [R] = conc. after time t
    When half of the reaction is completed, [R] = {tex}\frac {[R]_o}{2}{/tex}.
    Representing, the time taken for half of the reaction to be completed, by {tex}t_\frac 12{/tex}, equation becomes:
    {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t_\frac {1 }{ 2}} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{\frac {[ R ]_{ o }}{ 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 2{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
    The above equation shows that half-life of first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant.
  2. For a first order reaction
    {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
    {tex}t_{99 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 100{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303 \times 2}{k}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{4.606}{k}{/tex}
    and {tex}t_{90 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}} =2{/tex}
    {tex}t_{99 \%} =2 \times t_{90 \%}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The rate constant for the first order decomposition of H2O2 is given by the following equation:
{tex}\log k=14.2-\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} \mathrm{K}{/tex}
Calculate Ea for this reaction and rate constant k if its half-life period be 200 min. (Given, R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1)
question_text: The rate constant for the first order decomposition of H 2 O 2 is given by the following equation: {tex}\log k=14.2-\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} \mathrm{K}{/tex} Calculate E a for this reaction and rate constant k if its half-life period be 200 min. (Given, R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 )
explanation: Calculation of activation energy, E a : According to Arrhenius equation, k = Ae -E a /RT {tex}\log k=\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\log k=14.2-\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} {/tex} ...(ii) On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} {/tex} E a = 2.303R {tex}\times{/tex} 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 = 19.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 J mol -1 = 191.4 kJ mol -1 Calculation of rate constant, k: Given, t 1/2 = 200 min = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 sec {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{200 \times 60}{/tex} k = 5.775 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 s -1
solution: Calculation of activation energy, E a : According to Arrhenius equation, k = Ae -E a /RT {tex}\log k=\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\log k=14.2-\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} {/tex} ...(ii) On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} {/tex} E a = 2.303R {tex}\times{/tex} 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 = 19.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 J mol -1 = 191.4 kJ mol -1 Calculation of rate constant, k: Given, t 1/2 = 200 min = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 sec {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{200 \times 60}{/tex} k = 5.775 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 s -1
solution_html:
Calculation of activation energy, Ea:
According to Arrhenius equation,
k = Ae-Ea/RT
{tex}\log k=\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\log k=14.2-\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} {/tex} ...(ii)
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{1.0 \times 10^{4}}{T} {/tex}
Ea = 2.303R {tex}\times{/tex}1.0 {tex}\times{/tex}104
= 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex}1.0{tex}\times{/tex}104  {tex}\times{/tex}8.314
= 19.14 {tex}\times{/tex}104 J mol-1
= 191.4 kJ mol-1
Calculation of rate constant, k:
Given, t1/2 = 200 min = 200{tex}\times{/tex}60 sec
{tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{200 \times 60}{/tex}
k = 5.775 {tex}\times{/tex}10-5 s-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For a reaction: {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \stackrel{\mathrm{hv}}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{HCl}{/tex} Rate = k

  1. Write the order and molecularity of this reaction.
  2. Write the unit of k.
question_text: For a reaction: {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \stackrel{\mathrm{hv}}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{HCl}{/tex} Rate = k Write the order and molecularity of this reaction. Write the unit of k.
explanation: For a reaction, {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \stackrel{h v}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{HCl}{/tex} Rate= k, suggests that the reaction is of zero order. Further, the molecularity of a given reaction is 2 as two molecules are participating in the reaction. Hence, order = zero and molecularity = two. The unit of k for zero order reaction is equal to the rate of a reaction which is mol L -1 s -1 . Hence, the unit of k for the given reaction is mol L -1 s -1 .

question_text: For the first order thermal decomposition reaction, the following data obtained: C 2 H 5 CI(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 2 H 4 (g) + HCI(g) Time/s Total pressure/atm 0 0.30 300 0.50 Calculate the rate constant. (Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771, log 4 = 0.6021)

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define - Rate of reaction and the factors affecting the rate of reaction.
question_text: Define - Rate of reaction and the factors affecting the rate of reaction.
explanation: The reaction rate for a given chemical reaction is the measure of the change in concentration of the reactants or the change in concentration of the products per unit time. Chemical kinetics of a reaction depend on various factors such as reactant concentrations, temperature, physical states and surface areas of reactants, and also on solvent and catalyst properties if either are present.
solution: The reaction rate for a given chemical reaction is the measure of the change in concentration of the reactants or the change in concentration of the products per unit time. Chemical kinetics of a reaction depend on various factors such as reactant concentrations, temperature, physical states and surface areas of reactants, and also on solvent and catalyst properties if either are present.
solution_html: The reaction rate for a given chemical reaction is the measure of the change in concentration of the reactants or the change in concentration of the products per unit time. Chemical kinetics of a reaction depend on various factors such as reactant concentrations, temperature, physical states and surface areas of reactants, and also on solvent and catalyst properties if either are present.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The rate constants of a reaction at 500 K and 700 K are 0.02 s-1 and 0.07 s-1 respectively. Calculate the value of activation energy, Ea (R = 8. 314 J K-1 mol-1)
question_text: The rate constants of a reaction at 500 K and 700 K are 0.02 s -1 and 0.07 s -1 respectively. Calculate the value of activation energy, E a (R = 8. 314 J K -1 mol -1 )
explanation: Given, k 1 = 0.02 s -1 , k 2 = 0.07 s -1 , T 1 = 500 K, T 2 = 700 K, R = 8.314 J K -1 mol -1 Using Arrhenius equation, {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} \times T_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{0.07}{0.02}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{700-500}{500 \times 700}\right]{/tex} E a = 18230.35 J mol -1
solution: Given, k 1 = 0.02 s -1 , k 2 = 0.07 s -1 , T 1 = 500 K, T 2 = 700 K, R = 8.314 J K -1 mol -1 Using Arrhenius equation, {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} \times T_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{0.07}{0.02}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{700-500}{500 \times 700}\right]{/tex} E a = 18230.35 J mol -1
solution_html:
Given, k1 = 0.02 s-1, k2 = 0.07 s-1, T1 = 500 K, T2 = 700 K, R = 8.314 J K-1 mol-1
Using Arrhenius equation,
{tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} \times T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{0.07}{0.02}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{700-500}{500 \times 700}\right]{/tex}
Ea = 18230.35 J mol-1

question_html:
For the hydrolysis of methyl acetate in aqueous solution, the following results were obtained.

t/s 0 10 20
[CH3COOCH3] mol L-1 0.10 0.05 0.025
  1. Show that it follows pseudo first order reaction, as the concentration of water remains constant.
  2. Calculate the average rate of reaction between the time interval 10 to 20 s.
    (Given: log 2= 0.3010, log 4= 0.6021)
question_text: For the hydrolysis of methyl acetate in aqueous solution, the following results were obtained. t/s 0 10 20 [CH 3 COOCH 3] mol L -1 0.10 0.05 0.025 Show that it follows pseudo first order reaction, as the concentration of water remains constant. Calculate the average rate of reaction between the time interval 10 to 20 s. (Given: log 2= 0.3010, log 4= 0.6021)
explanation: For the hydrolysis of methyl acetate to be a pseudo first order reaction, the reaction should be first order with respect to ester when [H 2 O] is constant. The rate constant (k) for a first order reaction is given by {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} where, [R] 0 = initial concentration of the reactant [R] = final concentration of the reactant At t 1 = 10 s {tex}k_{1}=\frac{2.303}{10} \log \frac{0.10}{0.05}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} At t 2 = 20 s {tex}k_{2}=\frac{2.303}{20} \log \frac{0.10}{0.025}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} It can be seen that the rate constant (k) for the reaction has a constant value under any given time interval. Hence, the given reaction follows the pseudo first order kinetics. Average rate = {tex}\frac{-\Delta[\text { Ester }]}{\Delta t}=-\left(\frac{0.025-0.05}{20-10}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.025}{10}=0.0025 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: For the hydrolysis of methyl acetate to be a pseudo first order reaction, the reaction should be first order with respect to ester when [H 2 O] is constant. The rate constant (k) for a first order reaction is given by {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} where, [R] 0 = initial concentration of the reactant [R] = final concentration of the reactant At t 1 = 10 s {tex}k_{1}=\frac{2.303}{10} \log \frac{0.10}{0.05}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} At t 2 = 20 s {tex}k_{2}=\frac{2.303}{20} \log \frac{0.10}{0.025}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} It can be seen that the rate constant (k) for the reaction has a constant value under any given time interval. Hence, the given reaction follows the pseudo first order kinetics. Average rate = {tex}\frac{-\Delta[\text { Ester }]}{\Delta t}=-\left(\frac{0.025-0.05}{20-10}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.025}{10}=0.0025 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. For the hydrolysis of methyl acetate to be a pseudo first order reaction, the reaction should be first order with respect to ester when [H2O] is constant. The rate constant (k) for a first order reaction is given by
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    where, [R]0 = initial concentration of the reactant
    [R] = final concentration of the reactant
    At t1 = 10 s
    {tex}k_{1}=\frac{2.303}{10} \log \frac{0.10}{0.05}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
    At t2 = 20 s
    {tex}k_{2}=\frac{2.303}{20} \log \frac{0.10}{0.025}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
  2. It can be seen that the rate constant (k) for the reaction has a constant value under any given time interval. Hence, the given reaction follows the pseudo first order kinetics.
    Average rate = {tex}\frac{-\Delta[\text { Ester }]}{\Delta t}=-\left(\frac{0.025-0.05}{20-10}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{0.025}{10}=0.0025 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. For a reaction, A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P, the rate is given by Rate= k [A] [B]2
    1. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of B is doubled?
    2. What is the overall order of reaction if A is present in large excess?
  2. A first order reaction takes 30 min for 50% completion. Calculate the time required for 90% completion of this reaction. (log2 = 0.3010)
question_text: For a reaction, A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P, the rate is given by Rate= k [A] [B] 2 How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of B is doubled? What is the overall order of reaction if A is present in large excess? A first order reaction takes 30 min for 50% completion. Calculate the time required for 90% completion of this reaction. (log2 = 0.3010)
explanation: A + B {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P Rate = k [A][B 2 (given) If concentration of B is doubled, then rate of reaction= k[A][2B] 2 =4k[A][B] 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate becomes4 times the original rate. If A is present in large excess, then the reaction will be independent of the concentration of A and will be dependent only on the concentration of B. As [B] 2 will be the only determining factor in the order of reaction, the overall order of the reaction will be two. For the given first order reaction, the rate constant for 50% completion is given by {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} Here, t = time taken for 50% completion = 30 min [R] 0 =initial concentration of reactant [R] =final concentration of reactant Let [R] 0 be 100 and due to 50% completion of reaction, [R ] will be 100 - 50,i.e. 50 Putting values m (i), we get {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{30} \log \frac{100}{50}=\frac{2.303}{30} \log 2=0.023 \min ^{-1}{/tex} For same reaction the time required for 90% completion of reaction can be computed using the expression. {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} Here, [R] = final concentration of reactant = 100 - 90 = 10 {tex}0.023=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{2303}{0.023} \log 10=100.13 \mathrm{min}{/tex} Therefore, the time required for 90% completion of the given first order reaction is 100.13 min.
solution: A + B {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P Rate = k [A][B 2 (given) If concentration of B is doubled, then rate of reaction= k[A][2B] 2 =4k[A][B] 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate becomes4 times the original rate. If A is present in large excess, then the reaction will be independent of the concentration of A and will be dependent only on the concentration of B. As [B] 2 will be the only determining factor in the order of reaction, the overall order of the reaction will be two. For the given first order reaction, the rate constant for 50% completion is given by {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} Here, t = time taken for 50% completion = 30 min [R] 0 =initial concentration of reactant [R] =final concentration of reactant Let [R] 0 be 100 and due to 50% completion of reaction, [R ] will be 100 - 50,i.e. 50 Putting values m (i), we get {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{30} \log \frac{100}{50}=\frac{2.303}{30} \log 2=0.023 \min ^{-1}{/tex} For same reaction the time required for 90% completion of reaction can be computed using the expression. {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} Here, [R] = final concentration of reactant = 100 - 90 = 10 {tex}0.023=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{2303}{0.023} \log 10=100.13 \mathrm{min}{/tex} Therefore, the time required for 90% completion of the given first order reaction is 100.13 min.
solution_html:
1. A + B {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P
    Rate = k [A][B2 (given)
    1. If concentration of B is doubled, then rate of
      reaction= k[A][2B]2=4k[A][B]2
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate becomes4 times the original rate.
    2. If A is present in large excess, then the reaction will be independent of the concentration of A and will be dependent only on the concentration of B. As [B]2 will be the only determining factor in the order of reaction, the overall order of the reaction will be two.
  2. For the given first order reaction, the rate constant for 50% completion is given by
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    Here, t = time taken for 50% completion = 30 min
    [R]0 =initial concentration of reactant
    [R] =final concentration of reactant
    Let [R]0 be 100 and due to 50% completion of
    reaction, [R ] will be
    100 - 50,i.e. 50
    Putting values m (i), we get
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{30} \log \frac{100}{50}=\frac{2.303}{30} \log 2=0.023 \min ^{-1}{/tex}
    For same reaction the time required for 90% completion of reaction can be computed using the expression.
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    Here, [R] = final concentration of reactant
    = 100 - 90 = 10
    {tex}0.023=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{2303}{0.023} \log 10=100.13 \mathrm{min}{/tex}
    Therefore, the time required for 90% completion of the given first order reaction is
    100.13 min.
