The rate constant (k) for a first order reaction is given by {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} where, [R] 0 = initial concentration of the reactant [R] = final concentration of the reactant At t 1 = 10 s {tex}k_{1}=\frac{2.303}{10} \log \frac{0.10}{0.05}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} At t 2 = 20 s {tex}k_{2}=\frac{2.303}{20} \log \frac{0.10}{0.025}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} 0.005 mol L -1 s -1 It can be seen that the rate constant (k) for the reaction has a constant value under any given time interval. Hence, the given reaction follows the pseudo first order kinetics. {tex}\text { Average rate }=\frac{-\Delta[\text { Ester }]}{\Delta t}=-\left(\frac{0.025-0.05}{20-10}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.025}{10}=0.0025 \ \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. 2.3{tex}\times{/tex}10-2s-1
    For the hydrolysis of methyl acetate to be a pseudo first order reaction, the reaction should be first order with respect to ester when [H2O] is constant. The rate constant (k) for a first order reaction is given by
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    where, [R]0 = initial concentration of the reactant
    [R] = final concentration of the reactant
    At t1 = 10 s
    {tex}k_{1}=\frac{2.303}{10} \log \frac{0.10}{0.05}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
    At t2 = 20 s
    {tex}k_{2}=\frac{2.303}{20} \log \frac{0.10}{0.025}=6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
  2. 0.005 mol L-1 s-1 
    It can be seen that the rate constant (k) for the reaction has a constant value under any given time interval. Hence, the given reaction follows the pseudo first order kinetics.
    {tex}\text { Average rate }=\frac{-\Delta[\text { Ester }]}{\Delta t}=-\left(\frac{0.025-0.05}{20-10}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{0.025}{10}=0.0025 \ \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. For a chemical reaction R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P, the variation in the concentration, ln [R] vs time(s) plot is given as
    1. Predict the order of the reaction.
    2. What is the slope of curve?
    3. Write the unit of rate constant for this reaction.
  2. Show that the time required for 99% completion is double of the time required for the completion of 90% reaction.
question_text: For a chemical reaction R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P, the variation in the concentration, ln [R] vs time(s) plot is given as Predict the order of the reaction. What is the slope of curve? Write the unit of rate constant for this reaction. Show that the time required for 99% completion is double of the time required for the completion of 90% reaction.
explanation: The first order rate constant is given by {tex}k=\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} where, [R] 0 is the initial concentration of reactant and [R) is the concentration of reactant at time t. {tex}\therefore{/tex} In [R] = In[R]0 - kt y = c + mx The order of the reaction is first order. Slope of the curve = - k. Unit of rate constant for first order reaction = time-1 or s1 or min-1. Let [R] 0 = 100 For the reaction which is 99% completed, [R] = 1% of [R) 0 =I and for the reaction which is 90% completed, [R]= 10% of [R]o= 10. {tex}\therefore \quad k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{2.303}{t_{998}} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t_{998}} \log \left(10^{2}\right){/tex} ...(i) {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t_{90 \%}} \log \frac{100}{10}=\frac{2.303}{t_{90 \%}} \log (10){/tex} ...(ii) Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{2.303}{t_{999}} \log \left(10^{2}\right)=\frac{2.303}{t_{908}} \log (10){/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{t_{99 \%}}=\frac{1}{t_{90 \%}} \Rightarrow t_{99 \%}=2 t_{90 \%}{/tex} Hence, time required for 99% completion of a reaction of first order is double the time required for 90% completion of the reaction.
solution: The first order rate constant is given by {tex}k=\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} where, [R] 0 is the initial concentration of reactant and [R) is the concentration of reactant at time t. {tex}\therefore{/tex} In [R] = In[R]0 - kt y = c + mx The order of the reaction is first order. Slope of the curve = - k. Unit of rate constant for first order reaction = time-1 or s1 or min-1. Let [R] 0 = 100 For the reaction which is 99% completed, [R] = 1% of [R) 0 =I and for the reaction which is 90% completed, [R]= 10% of [R]o= 10. {tex}\therefore \quad k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{2.303}{t_{998}} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t_{998}} \log \left(10^{2}\right){/tex} ...(i) {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t_{90 \%}} \log \frac{100}{10}=\frac{2.303}{t_{90 \%}} \log (10){/tex} ...(ii) Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{2.303}{t_{999}} \log \left(10^{2}\right)=\frac{2.303}{t_{908}} \log (10){/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{t_{99 \%}}=\frac{1}{t_{90 \%}} \Rightarrow t_{99 \%}=2 t_{90 \%}{/tex} Hence, time required for 99% completion of a reaction of first order is double the time required for 90% completion of the reaction.
solution_html:
1. The first order rate constant is given by
    {tex}k=\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} where, [R]0 is the initial
    concentration of reactant and [R) is the
    concentration of reactant at time t.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} In [R] = In[R]0 - kt
    y = c + mx
    1. The order of the reaction is first order.
    2. Slope of the curve = - k.
    3. Unit of rate constant for first order reaction
      = time-1 or s1 or min-1.
  2. Let [R]0 = 100
    For the reaction which is 99% completed,
    [R] = 1% of [R)0 =I and for the reaction which is
    90% completed, [R]= 10% of [R]o= 10.
    {tex}\therefore \quad k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{2.303}{t_{998}} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t_{998}} \log \left(10^{2}\right){/tex} ...(i)
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} 
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t_{90 \%}} \log \frac{100}{10}=\frac{2.303}{t_{90 \%}} \log (10){/tex}...(ii)
    Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii)
    {tex}\frac{2.303}{t_{999}} \log \left(10^{2}\right)=\frac{2.303}{t_{908}} \log (10){/tex}
    {tex}\frac{2}{t_{99 \%}}=\frac{1}{t_{90 \%}} \Rightarrow t_{99 \%}=2 t_{90 \%}{/tex}
    Hence, time required for 99% completion of a reaction of first order is double the time required for 90% completion of the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A reaction is second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is:

  1. doubled
  2. reduced to half
question_text: A reaction is second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is: doubled reduced to half
explanation: Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a Rate of reaction, {tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} = k{a^2}{/tex} If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex} = 4R Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e., {tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex} .
solution: Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a Rate of reaction, {tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} = k{a^2}{/tex} If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex} = 4R Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e., {tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a
Rate of reaction,{tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex}{tex} = k{a^2}{/tex}

  1. If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be
    {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex}
    = 4R
    Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times.
  2. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e.,{tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} 
    Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Hydrogen peroxide, {tex}H_2O_2(aq){/tex} decomposes to {tex}H_2O{/tex} and {tex}O_2{/tex} in a reaction which is first order in {tex}H_2O_2{/tex} and has a rate constant, {tex}k = 1.06 \times {10}^{-3} {min}^{-1}.{/tex}Then:

  1. How long will it take 15% of a sample of {tex}H_2O_2{/tex} to decompose?
  2. How long will it take 85% of a sample of {tex}H_2O_2{/tex} to decompose?
question_text: Hydrogen peroxide, {tex}H_2O_2(aq){/tex} decomposes to {tex}H_2O{/tex} and {tex}O_2{/tex} in a reaction which is first order in {tex}H_2O_2{/tex} and has a rate constant, {tex}k = 1.06 \times {10}^{-3} {min}^{-1}.{/tex} Then: How long will it take 15% of a sample of {tex}H_2O_2{/tex} to decompose? How long will it take 85% of a sample of {tex}H_2O_2{/tex} to decompose?
explanation: For 15% decomposition of {tex}H_20_2{/tex} [R] 0 = 100 M, [R] = 100 - 15 = 85 M k = 1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 min -1 For first order reaction {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{[{R_0}]}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times \log \left[ {\frac{{100}}{{85}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times [\log 100 - \log 85]{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times \log (2 - 1.9292){/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times (0.0706){/tex} t = 153.38 min. Similarly, for 85% decomposition of reaction {tex}t = \left( {\frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} \right)\log \left( {\frac{{100}}{{15}}} \right){/tex} t = 1790.325 min.
solution: For 15% decomposition of {tex}H_20_2{/tex} [R] 0 = 100 M, [R] = 100 - 15 = 85 M k = 1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 min -1 For first order reaction {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{[{R_0}]}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times \log \left[ {\frac{{100}}{{85}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times [\log 100 - \log 85]{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times \log (2 - 1.9292){/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times (0.0706){/tex} t = 153.38 min. Similarly, for 85% decomposition of reaction {tex}t = \left( {\frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} \right)\log \left( {\frac{{100}}{{15}}} \right){/tex} t = 1790.325 min.
solution_html:
1. For 15% decomposition of {tex}H_20_2{/tex}
    [R]0 = 100 M, [R] = 100 - 15 = 85 M
    k = 1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 min-1
    For first order reaction
    {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{[{R_0}]}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
    {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times \log \left[ {\frac{{100}}{{85}}} \right]{/tex}
    {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times [\log 100 - \log 85]{/tex}
    {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times \log (2 - 1.9292){/tex}
    {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times (0.0706){/tex}
    t = 153.38 min.
  2. Similarly, for 85% decomposition of reaction
    {tex}t = \left( {\frac{{2.303}}{{1.06 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} \right)\log \left( {\frac{{100}}{{15}}} \right){/tex}
    t = 1790.325 min.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For a decomposition reaction the values of rate constant K at two different temperatures are given below:
{tex}{K_1} = 2.15 \times {10^{ - 8}}L\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}at 650 K
{tex}{K_2} = 2.39 \times {10^{ - 7}}L\,\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}at 700 K
Calculate the value of activation energy for this reaction. (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1)
question_text: For a decomposition reaction the values of rate constant K at two different temperatures are given below: {tex}{K_1} = 2.15 \times {10^{ - 8}}L\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} at 650 K {tex}{K_2} = 2.39 \times {10^{ - 7}}L\,\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} at 700 K Calculate the value of activation energy for this reaction. (R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 )
solution_html:
Here {tex}{K_1} = 2.15 \times {10^{ - 8}}L\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\,{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{K_2} = 2.39 \times {10^{ - 7}}L\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\,{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{T_1} = 650\,K,\,{T_2} = 700K{/tex}and
{tex}R = 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Using the formula
{tex}\log \frac{{{K_2}}}{{{K_1}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\log = \frac{{2.39 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{2.15 \times {{10}^{ - 8}}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{700 - 650}}{{650 \times 700}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\log 1.11 \times 10 = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{19.147}} \times \frac{{50}}{{455000}}{/tex}
{tex}1.0457 = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{19.147}} \times \frac{1}{{9100}}{/tex}
Ea = 182200.365 J mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: How does a change in temperature affect the rate of a reaction? How can this effect on the rate constant of a reaction be represented quantitatively?
question_text: How does a change in temperature affect the rate of a reaction? How can this effect on the rate constant of a reaction be represented quantitatively?
explanation: The rate of reaction increases with temperature. The temperature coefficient is the ratio of rate constant at temperature (T + 10) K to the rate constant at temperature TK. Temperature coefficient {tex}=\frac{\text { Rate constant at }(T+10) \mathrm{K}}{\text { Rate constant at } T \mathrm{K}}{/tex} It is observed that for a chemical reaction with rise in temperature by 10°, the rate constant is nearly doubled.
solution: The rate of reaction increases with temperature. The temperature coefficient is the ratio of rate constant at temperature (T + 10) K to the rate constant at temperature TK. Temperature coefficient {tex}=\frac{\text { Rate constant at }(T+10) \mathrm{K}}{\text { Rate constant at } T \mathrm{K}}{/tex} It is observed that for a chemical reaction with rise in temperature by 10°, the rate constant is nearly doubled.
solution_html:
The rate of reaction increases with temperature. The temperature coefficient is the ratio of rate constant at temperature (T + 10) K to the rate constant at temperature TK.
Temperature coefficient {tex}=\frac{\text { Rate constant at }(T+10) \mathrm{K}}{\text { Rate constant at } T \mathrm{K}}{/tex}
It is observed that for a chemical reaction with rise in temperature by 10°, the rate constant is nearly doubled.

question_html:
What do you understand by the rate law and rate constant of a reaction? Identify the order of a reaction if the units of its rate constant are:

  1. L-1 mol s-1
  2. L mol-1 s-1
question_text: What do you understand by the rate law and rate constant of a reaction? Identify the order of a reaction if the units of its rate constant are: L -1 mol s -1 L mol -1 s -1
explanation: Rate law is expression which gives the relationship between the rate of reaction and concentration of reactants. {tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = k [ \mathrm { A } ] ^ { x } [ \mathrm { B } \mathrm { P }] ^ { y }{/tex} Rate constant is equal to the rate of reaction when conc. of reactants is equal to unity. 'k' is rate constant is above expression. Zero-order Second order
solution: Rate law is expression which gives the relationship between the rate of reaction and concentration of reactants. {tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = k [ \mathrm { A } ] ^ { x } [ \mathrm { B } \mathrm { P }] ^ { y }{/tex} Rate constant is equal to the rate of reaction when conc. of reactants is equal to unity. 'k' is rate constant is above expression. Zero-order Second order
solution_html:
Rate law is expression which gives the relationship between the rate of reaction and concentration of reactants.

{tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = k [ \mathrm { A } ] ^ { x } [ \mathrm { B } \mathrm { P }] ^ { y }{/tex}

Rate constant is equal to the rate of reaction when conc. of reactants is equal to unity. 'k' is rate constant is above expression.

  1. Zero-order
  2. Second order

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A reaction is of second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected, if the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half? What is the unit of rate constant for such a reaction?
question_text: A reaction is of second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected, if the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half? What is the unit of rate constant for such a reaction?
explanation: When the concentration rs reduced to half {tex}R^{*}=\frac{R}{2}{/tex} New rate, {tex}r^{\prime \prime} \propto\left[R^{\prime \prime}\right]^{2} \propto\left[\frac{R}{2}\right]^{2}=\frac{1}{4}[R]^{2} {/tex} ....... (iii) From Eqs. (i) and (iii), {tex}\frac{r}{r^{\prime \prime}}=\frac{[R]^{2}}{\frac{1}{4}[R]^{2}}=4{/tex} Or {tex}r^{\prime \prime}=\frac{r}{4}{/tex} Hence, the rate becomes one fourth of the initial rate when concentration of the reactant is reduced to half. The unit of rate constant is L mol -1 s -1 (second order reaction).
solution: When the concentration rs reduced to half {tex}R^{*}=\frac{R}{2}{/tex} New rate, {tex}r^{\prime \prime} \propto\left[R^{\prime \prime}\right]^{2} \propto\left[\frac{R}{2}\right]^{2}=\frac{1}{4}[R]^{2} {/tex} ....... (iii) From Eqs. (i) and (iii), {tex}\frac{r}{r^{\prime \prime}}=\frac{[R]^{2}}{\frac{1}{4}[R]^{2}}=4{/tex} Or {tex}r^{\prime \prime}=\frac{r}{4}{/tex} Hence, the rate becomes one fourth of the initial rate when concentration of the reactant is reduced to half. The unit of rate constant is L mol -1 s -1 (second order reaction).
solution_html:
When the concentration rs reduced to half
{tex}R^{*}=\frac{R}{2}{/tex}
New rate, {tex}r^{\prime \prime} \propto\left[R^{\prime \prime}\right]^{2} \propto\left[\frac{R}{2}\right]^{2}=\frac{1}{4}[R]^{2} {/tex} ....... (iii)
From Eqs. (i) and (iii),
{tex}\frac{r}{r^{\prime \prime}}=\frac{[R]^{2}}{\frac{1}{4}[R]^{2}}=4{/tex}
Or {tex}r^{\prime \prime}=\frac{r}{4}{/tex}
Hence, the rate becomes one fourth of the initial rate when concentration of the reactant is reduced to half.
The unit of rate constant is L mol-1 s-1
(second order reaction).

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. For a reaction, A + B {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Product, The rate law is given by, Rate= k [A]1 [B]2. What is the order of the reaction?
  2. Write the unit of rate constant 'k' for the first order reaction.
question_text: For a reaction, A + B {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Product, The rate law is given by, Rate= k [A] 1 [B] 2 . What is the order of the reaction? Write the unit of rate constant 'k' for the first order reaction.
explanation: The sum of the powers of the concentrations of reactants in the rate law expression is called order of that chemical reaction. Rate= k[A] 1 [B] 2 Order of reaction = (1+ 2) = 3 Unit of rate constant for first order reaction is s -1 or min -1 or time -1 .
solution: The sum of the powers of the concentrations of reactants in the rate law expression is called order of that chemical reaction. Rate= k[A] 1 [B] 2 Order of reaction = (1+ 2) = 3 Unit of rate constant for first order reaction is s -1 or min -1 or time -1 .
solution_html:
1. The sum of the powers of the concentrations of reactants in the rate law expression is called order of that chemical reaction.
    Rate= k[A]1 [B]2
    Order of reaction = (1+ 2) = 3
  2. Unit of rate constant for first order reaction is s-1 or min-1 or time-1.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For a chemical reaction, R {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P, the variation in the concentration of R versus time (t) plot is given as

  1. Predict the order of the reaction.
  2. What is the slope of the curve?
question_text: For a chemical reaction, R {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P, the variation in the concentration of R versus time (t) plot is given as Predict the order of the reaction. What is the slope of the curve?
explanation: The reaction is of zero order. For zero order reaction, [R] =-kt + [R] 0 Compare with equation of a straight line (y = mx + c). If we plot [R] against t, we get a straight line with slope= -k and intercept equal to [R] 0 .
solution: The reaction is of zero order. For zero order reaction, [R] =-kt + [R] 0 Compare with equation of a straight line (y = mx + c). If we plot [R] against t, we get a straight line with slope= -k and intercept equal to [R] 0 .
solution_html:
1. The reaction is of zero order.
  2. For zero order reaction, [R] =-kt + [R]0
    Compare with equation of a straight line (y = mx + c). If we plot [R] against t, we get a straight line with slope= -k and intercept equal to [R]0.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Define half-life of a reaction. Write the expression of half-life for

  1. zero order reaction
  2. first order reaction
question_text: Define half-life of a reaction. Write the expression of half-life for zero order reaction first order reaction
explanation: Half-life (t 1/2 ) of a reaction is the time in which the concentration of reactant is reduced to one half of its initial concentration. For a zero order reaction, {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{[R]_{0}}{2 k}{/tex} For first order reaction, {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
solution: Half-life (t 1/2 ) of a reaction is the time in which the concentration of reactant is reduced to one half of its initial concentration. For a zero order reaction, {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{[R]_{0}}{2 k}{/tex} For first order reaction, {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
Half-life (t1/2) of a reaction is the time in which the concentration of reactant is reduced to one half of its initial concentration.

  1. For a zero order reaction, {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{[R]_{0}}{2 k}{/tex}
  2. For first order reaction, {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Define the following terms:

  1. Rate constant (k) 
  2. Activation energy (Ea)
question_text: Define the following terms: Rate constant (k) Activation energy (E a )
explanation: Rate constant is the coefficient of proportionality related to the rate of reaction at a given temperature to the concentrations of reactants. It is the extra energy contained by reactant molecules that results into effective collisions between them to form the products. It is denoted by E a .

question_html:
The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of SO2CL2 at a constant volume:
SO2CI2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO2(g) + CI2(g)

Experiment Time Total pressure/atm
1 0 0.4
2 100 0.7

Calculate the rate constant.
(Given, log 4 = 0.6021, log 2 = 0.3010)
question_text: The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of SO 2 CL 2 at a constant volume: SO 2 CI 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO 2 (g) + CI 2 (g) Experiment Time Total pressure/atm 1 0 0.4 2 100 0.7 Calculate the rate constant. (Given, log 4 = 0.6021, log 2 = 0.3010)
explanation: SO 2 CI 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO 2 (g) + CI 2 (g) Initial pressure p i 0 0 After time, t p i - p p p Total pressure after time t, i.e. p t = p i - p + p + p = p i + p {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p t - p i Thus, a = p i and a - x = p i - p = p i - (p t - p i ) = p i - p t + p i = 2p i - p i Substituting the values of a and (a - x) in equation, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{p_{1}}{\left(2 p_{t}-p_{t}\right)}{/tex} Calculating rate constant (k), when t = 100s Given, P t = 04 atm and e, = 0.7 atm Then, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{p_{t}}{\left(2 p_{t}-p_{t}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{0.4}{(2 \times 0.4-0.7)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{100} \log \frac{0.4}{0.1}=\frac{2.303}{100} \log 4{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2303}{100} \times 06021{/tex} = 0.01387 = 1.387 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s -1
solution: SO 2 CI 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO 2 (g) + CI 2 (g) Initial pressure p i 0 0 After time, t p i - p p p Total pressure after time t, i.e. p t = p i - p + p + p = p i + p {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p t - p i Thus, a = p i and a - x = p i - p = p i - (p t - p i ) = p i - p t + p i = 2p i - p i Substituting the values of a and (a - x) in equation, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{p_{1}}{\left(2 p_{t}-p_{t}\right)}{/tex} Calculating rate constant (k), when t = 100s Given, P t = 04 atm and e, = 0.7 atm Then, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{p_{t}}{\left(2 p_{t}-p_{t}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{0.4}{(2 \times 0.4-0.7)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{100} \log \frac{0.4}{0.1}=\frac{2.303}{100} \log 4{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2303}{100} \times 06021{/tex} = 0.01387 = 1.387 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s -1
solution_html:
SO2CI2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}SO2(g) + CI2(g)

Initial pressure pi 0 0
After time, t pi -  p p p

Total pressure after time t,
i.e. pt = pi - p + p + p = pi + p
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = pt - pi
Thus, a = pi and a - x = pi - p = pi - (pt - pi)
= pi - pt + pi = 2pi - pi
Substituting the values of a and (a - x) in equation,
{tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{p_{1}}{\left(2 p_{t}-p_{t}\right)}{/tex}
Calculating rate constant (k), when t = 100s
Given, Pt = 04 atm and e, = 0.7 atm
Then, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{p_{t}}{\left(2 p_{t}-p_{t}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{0.4}{(2 \times 0.4-0.7)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{100} \log \frac{0.4}{0.1}=\frac{2.303}{100} \log 4{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2303}{100} \times 06021{/tex} = 0.01387 = 1.387{tex}\times{/tex}10-2s-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A reaction is first order in A and second order in B.

  1. Write the differential rate equation.
  2. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of B three times?
  3. How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both A and B are doubled?
question_text: A reaction is first order in A and second order in B. Write the differential rate equation. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of B three times? How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both A and B are doubled?
explanation: It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B. The differential rate equation will be {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} If the concentration of B is increased three times, then {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled, {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.
solution: It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B. The differential rate equation will be {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} If the concentration of B is increased three times, then {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled, {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.
solution_html:
It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B.

  1. The differential rate equation will be
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
  2. If the concentration of B is increased three times, then
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
    Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times.
  3. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled,
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
    Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.

explanation: First, find out the value of a - x from the given percentage, then the value of k by using the formula, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} Again find out the value of a - x from the given percentage then the value of t by using the formula {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} and keeping the value of k in this formula For the first order reaction, when 1 = 100 mm rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} For 60% completion of the reaction, if a = 100% a - x = 100 - 60 = 40% Then, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex} ..... (i) For 90% completion of the reaction, a = 100% and a - x = 100 - 90 = 10% Then, {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} ..... (ii) Substituting the value of k in Eq. (ii), we have, {tex}\frac{2.303}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}=\frac{2.303}{t} \quad[\because \log 10=1]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{t}=\frac{1}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{t}=\frac{0.3979}{100} \Rightarrow t=\frac{100}{0.3979}{/tex} t = 251.2 min
solution: First, find out the value of a - x from the given percentage, then the value of k by using the formula, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} Again find out the value of a - x from the given percentage then the value of t by using the formula {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} and keeping the value of k in this formula For the first order reaction, when 1 = 100 mm rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} For 60% completion of the reaction, if a = 100% a - x = 100 - 60 = 40% Then, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex} ..... (i) For 90% completion of the reaction, a = 100% and a - x = 100 - 90 = 10% Then, {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} ..... (ii) Substituting the value of k in Eq. (ii), we have, {tex}\frac{2.303}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}=\frac{2.303}{t} \quad[\because \log 10=1]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{t}=\frac{1}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{t}=\frac{0.3979}{100} \Rightarrow t=\frac{100}{0.3979}{/tex} t = 251.2 min
solution_html:
1. First, find out the value of a - x from the given percentage, then the value of k by using the formula,
    {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
  2. Again find out the value of a - x from the given percentage then the value of t by using the formula
    {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
    and keeping the value of k in this formula
    For the first order reaction, when 1 = 100 mm
    rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
    For 60% completion of the reaction,
    if a = 100%
    a - x = 100 - 60 = 40%
    Then, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex} ..... (i)
     For 90% completion of the reaction,
    a = 100%
    and a - x = 100 - 90 = 10%
    Then, {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} ..... (ii)
    Substituting the value of k in Eq. (ii), we have,
    {tex}\frac{2.303}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}=\frac{2.303}{t} \quad[\because \log 10=1]{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{1}{t}=\frac{1}{100} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{t}=\frac{0.3979}{100} \Rightarrow t=\frac{100}{0.3979}{/tex}
    t = 251.2 min

question_html:
Rate constant 'k' of a reaction varies with temperature 'T' according to the equation

question_text: Rate constant 'k' of a reaction varies with temperature 'T' according to the equation {tex}\log k=\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right){/tex} where E a is the activation energy. When a graph is plotted for log k vs {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} , a straight line with a slope of -4250 K is obtained. Calculate 'E a ' for the reaction. (R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 )
explanation: Given, Slope= - 4250 K, R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 From Arrhenius equation, log k = {tex}\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}{/tex} Comparing with straight line equation, y = mx + c {tex}-\frac{E_{a}}{2303 R}=-4250{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E a = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 4250 = 81.37 kJmol -1
solution: Given, Slope= - 4250 K, R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 From Arrhenius equation, log k = {tex}\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}{/tex} Comparing with straight line equation, y = mx + c {tex}-\frac{E_{a}}{2303 R}=-4250{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E a = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 4250 = 81.37 kJmol -1

Given, Slope= - 4250 K, R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1
From Arrhenius equation,
log k = {tex}\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}{/tex}
Comparing with straight line equation, y = mx + c
{tex}-\frac{E_{a}}{2303 R}=-4250{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ea = 2.303{tex}\times{/tex}8.314{tex}\times{/tex}4250
= 81.37 kJmol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define activation energy of reaction.
question_text: Define activation energy of reaction.
explanation: Activation Energy (E a ) is the least amount of energy required to activate atoms or molecules to a state in which they can undergo a chemical reaction. Activation energy may also be defined as the minimum energy required to start a chemical reaction. It is independent of temperature and expressed in units of kilojoules per mole (kJ/mol) or kilocalories per mole (kcal/mol).

Calculate the value of rate constant for the reaction. Which of the following possible reaction mechanism is consistent with the rate law? {tex}{\rm{A + B}} \to {\rm{C + E}}\,{\rm{(slow)}}{/tex} {tex}A + E \to D\,\,(fast){/tex} {tex}B \to C + E\,(slow){/tex} {tex}A + E \to F\,\,(fast){/tex} {tex}A + F \to D\,\,(fast){/tex}
explanation: Let rate law is {tex}Rate = k{[A]^x}{[B]^y}{/tex} {tex}So, \,1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.1]^x}{[0.1]^y}\,...\,(i){/tex} {tex}3.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.2]^x}{[0.2]^y}\,...\,(ii){/tex} {tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.2]^x}{[0.4]^y}\,...\,(iii){/tex} From eq.(ii) and (iii) {tex}\frac{{6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = \frac{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.4]}^y}}}{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.2]}^y}}}{/tex} 2 y = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1 From eq.(i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = \frac{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.2]}^1}}}{{k{{[0.1]}^x}{{[0.1]}^1}}}{/tex} {tex}2 = {2^x} \times 2{/tex} 2 x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 Thus, the rate is given as Rate =k[B] 1 Rate = k[B] {tex}k = \frac{{Rate}}{{[B]}} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.2}}{/tex} {tex} = 15 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} B {tex}\to{/tex} C + E (slow) is the possible reaction with is consistent with the rate law i.e., {tex}Rate = k [B]^1.{/tex} Hence, mechanism II is appropriate for the reaction.
solution: Let rate law is {tex}Rate = k{[A]^x}{[B]^y}{/tex} {tex}So, \,1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.1]^x}{[0.1]^y}\,...\,(i){/tex} {tex}3.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.2]^x}{[0.2]^y}\,...\,(ii){/tex} {tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.2]^x}{[0.4]^y}\,...\,(iii){/tex} From eq.(ii) and (iii) {tex}\frac{{6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = \frac{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.4]}^y}}}{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.2]}^y}}}{/tex} 2 y = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1 From eq.(i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = \frac{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.2]}^1}}}{{k{{[0.1]}^x}{{[0.1]}^1}}}{/tex} {tex}2 = {2^x} \times 2{/tex} 2 x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 Thus, the rate is given as Rate =k[B] 1 Rate = k[B] {tex}k = \frac{{Rate}}{{[B]}} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.2}}{/tex} {tex} = 15 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} B {tex}\to{/tex} C + E (slow) is the possible reaction with is consistent with the rate law i.e., {tex}Rate = k [B]^1.{/tex} Hence, mechanism II is appropriate for the reaction.
solution_html:
1. Let rate law is
    {tex}Rate = k{[A]^x}{[B]^y}{/tex}
    {tex}So, \,1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.1]^x}{[0.1]^y}\,...\,(i){/tex}
    {tex}3.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.2]^x}{[0.2]^y}\,...\,(ii){/tex}
    {tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{[0.2]^x}{[0.4]^y}\,...\,(iii){/tex}
    From eq.(ii) and (iii)
    {tex}\frac{{6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = \frac{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.4]}^y}}}{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.2]}^y}}}{/tex}
    2y = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1
    From eq.(i) and (ii)
    {tex}\frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = \frac{{k{{[0.2]}^x}{{[0.2]}^1}}}{{k{{[0.1]}^x}{{[0.1]}^1}}}{/tex}
    {tex}2 = {2^x} \times 2{/tex}
    2x = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
    Thus, the rate is given as Rate =k[B]1
  2. Rate = k[B]
    {tex}k = \frac{{Rate}}{{[B]}} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.2}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 15 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
  3. B {tex}\to{/tex} C + E (slow) is the possible reaction with is consistent with the rate law i.e.,
    {tex}Rate = k [B]^1.{/tex}
    Hence, mechanism II is appropriate for the reaction.

question_html:

Exp. No. Initial [NO]
(M)		 Initial
[Cl2] (M)		 Initial rate of disappearance of Cl2 (M/min)

question_text: For the reaction 2NO (g) + Cl 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NOCl(g) The following data were collected. All the measurements were taken at 263 K. Exp. No. Initial [NO] (M) Initial [Cl 2 ] (M) Initial rate of disappearance of Cl 2 (M/min) 1. 0.15 0.15 0.60 2. 0.15 0.30 1.20 3. 0.30 0.15 2.40 4. 0.25 0.25 ? Write the expression for rate law. Calculate the value of rate constant and specify its unit. What is the initial rate of disappearance of Cl 2 in experiment 4?
explanation: Rate law may be written as Rate= k [NO] P [Cl 2 ] q The initial rate becomes (Rate) 0 = k [NO] P [Cl 2 ] q Comparing experiments 1 and 2, (Rate)1 = k (0.15) p (0.15) q = 0.60 ...(i) (Rate)2 = k (0.15) p (0.30) q = 1.20 ...(ii) Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), {tex}\frac{(\text { Rate })_{2}}{(\text { Rate })_{1}}=\frac{k(0.15)^{p}(0.30)^{q}}{k(0.15)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}=\frac{1.20}{0.60}{/tex} or 2 q = 2 l {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Order with respect to Cl 2 = 1 Comparing experiments 1 and 3, (Rate) 1 = k (0.15) p (0.15) q = 0.60 ...(iii) (Rate) 3 = k (0.30) p (0.15) q = 2.40 ...(iv) Dividing Eq. (iv) by Eq. (iii) {tex}\frac{(\text { Rate })_{3}}{(\text { Rate })_{1}}=\frac{k(0.30)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}{k(0.15)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}=\frac{2.40}{0.60}{/tex} or 2 p = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 p = 2 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 2 Thus, order with respect to NO is 2 Rate law= k [NO] 2 [Cl 2 ] The rate law for the given reaction Rate= k [NO] 2 [Cl 2 ] Rate constant can be calculated by substituting the value of rate, [NO]and [Cl 2 ] for any of the experiments {tex}k=\frac{\text { Rate }}{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}=\frac{0.60}{(0.15)^{2}(0.15)}=\frac{060}{0.00338}{/tex} = 17751 mol -2 L 2 min -1 Let initial rate of disapperance of CI 2 in experiment 4 is r 4 . {tex}\therefore{/tex} r4 = k [NO] 2 [Cl 2 ] = 177.51 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.25) 2 (0.25) = 2.78 M/min
solution: Rate law may be written as Rate= k [NO] P [Cl 2 ] q The initial rate becomes (Rate) 0 = k [NO] P [Cl 2 ] q Comparing experiments 1 and 2, (Rate)1 = k (0.15) p (0.15) q = 0.60 ...(i) (Rate)2 = k (0.15) p (0.30) q = 1.20 ...(ii) Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), {tex}\frac{(\text { Rate })_{2}}{(\text { Rate })_{1}}=\frac{k(0.15)^{p}(0.30)^{q}}{k(0.15)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}=\frac{1.20}{0.60}{/tex} or 2 q = 2 l {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Order with respect to Cl 2 = 1 Comparing experiments 1 and 3, (Rate) 1 = k (0.15) p (0.15) q = 0.60 ...(iii) (Rate) 3 = k (0.30) p (0.15) q = 2.40 ...(iv) Dividing Eq. (iv) by Eq. (iii) {tex}\frac{(\text { Rate })_{3}}{(\text { Rate })_{1}}=\frac{k(0.30)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}{k(0.15)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}=\frac{2.40}{0.60}{/tex} or 2 p = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 p = 2 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 2 Thus, order with respect to NO is 2 Rate law= k [NO] 2 [Cl 2 ] The rate law for the given reaction Rate= k [NO] 2 [Cl 2 ] Rate constant can be calculated by substituting the value of rate, [NO]and [Cl 2 ] for any of the experiments {tex}k=\frac{\text { Rate }}{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}=\frac{0.60}{(0.15)^{2}(0.15)}=\frac{060}{0.00338}{/tex} = 17751 mol -2 L 2 min -1 Let initial rate of disapperance of CI 2 in experiment 4 is r 4 . {tex}\therefore{/tex} r4 = k [NO] 2 [Cl 2 ] = 177.51 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.25) 2 (0.25) = 2.78 M/min
solution_html:
Rate law may be written as
Rate= k [NO]P [Cl2]q
The initial rate becomes
(Rate)0 = k [NO]P [Cl2]q
Comparing experiments 1 and 2,
(Rate)1 = k (0.15)p (0.15)q = 0.60 ...(i)
(Rate)2 = k (0.15)p (0.30)q = 1.20 ...(ii)
Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i),
{tex}\frac{(\text { Rate })_{2}}{(\text { Rate })_{1}}=\frac{k(0.15)^{p}(0.30)^{q}}{k(0.15)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}=\frac{1.20}{0.60}{/tex}
or 2q = 2l {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Order with respect to Cl2 = 1
Comparing experiments 1 and 3,
(Rate)1 = k (0.15)p (0.15)q = 0.60 ...(iii)
(Rate)3 = k (0.30)p (0.15)q = 2.40 ...(iv)
Dividing Eq. (iv) by Eq. (iii)
{tex}\frac{(\text { Rate })_{3}}{(\text { Rate })_{1}}=\frac{k(0.30)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}{k(0.15)^{p}(0.15)^{q}}=\frac{2.40}{0.60}{/tex}
or 2p = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}2p = 22
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 2
Thus, order with respect to NO is 2
Rate law= k [NO]2 [Cl2]

  1. The rate law for the given reaction
    Rate= k [NO]2[Cl2]
  2. Rate constant can be calculated by substituting the value of rate, [NO]and [Cl2] for any of the experiments
    {tex}k=\frac{\text { Rate }}{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}=\frac{0.60}{(0.15)^{2}(0.15)}=\frac{060}{0.00338}{/tex}
    = 17751 mol-2 L2 min-1
  3. Let initial rate of disapperance of CI2 in experiment 4 is r4.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} r4 = k [NO]2[Cl2] = 177.51 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.25)2 (0.25)
    = 2.78 M/min

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Explain the following terms:

  1. Rate determining step of a reaction.
  2. Molecularity of a reaction.
question_text: Explain the following terms: Rate determining step of a reaction. Molecularity of a reaction.
explanation: The slowest step in the reaction mechanism is the rate determining step of a reaction. S.No. Molecularity of a reaction Order of reaction (i) The number of reacting species which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of a reaction. The sum of powers of the concentrations of the reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. (ii) Molecularityis always a whole number value. Order may have zero, whole number, negative or fractional, values.
solution: The slowest step in the reaction mechanism is the rate determining step of a reaction. S.No. Molecularity of a reaction Order of reaction (i) The number of reacting species which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of a reaction. The sum of powers of the concentrations of the reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. (ii) Molecularityis always a whole number value. Order may have zero, whole number, negative or fractional, values.
solution_html:
1. The slowest step in the reaction mechanism is the rate determining step of a reaction.
  2. S.No. Molecularity of a reaction Order of reaction
    (i) The number of reacting species which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of a reaction. The sum of powers of the concentrations of the reactants in the rate law expression is called
    the order of that chemical reaction.
    (ii) Molecularityis always a whole number value. Order may have zero, whole number, negative or fractional, values.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Why does the rate of reaction not remain constant throughout the reaction process?
question_text: Why does the rate of reaction not remain constant throughout the reaction process?
explanation: Rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of reactant. As the reaction proceeds in forward direction concentration of reactant decreases, so rate of reaction decreases with respect to time.
solution: Rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of reactant. As the reaction proceeds in forward direction concentration of reactant decreases, so rate of reaction decreases with respect to time.
solution_html: Rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of reactant. As the reaction proceeds in forward direction concentration of reactant decreases, so rate of reaction decreases with respect to time.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A first order reaction has a rate constant of 0.0051 min-1. If we begin with 0.10 M concentration of the reactant what concentration of the reactant will be left after 3 hours?
question_text: A first order reaction has a rate constant of 0.0051 min -1 . If we begin with 0.10 M concentration of the reactant what concentration of the reactant will be left after 3 hours?
solution_html:
Here [R]0 = 0.10 M
t= 3 hours =180 min
k= 0.0051 min-1
Using the formula

{tex}K = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
{tex}0.0051 = \frac{{2.303}}{{180}}\log \frac{{0.10}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{{0.10}}{{[R]}} = \frac{{0.0051 \times 180}}{{2.303}}{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{{0.10}}{{[R]}} = \frac{{0.0051 \times 180}}{{2.303}}{/tex}= 0.3986
{tex}\therefore \frac{{0.10}}{{[R]}}{/tex} = antilog (0.3986)
{tex}\frac{{0.10}}{{[R]}} = 2.503 \Rightarrow [R]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.10}}{{2.503}}{/tex}= 0.309 M

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Derive general form of the expression for half life period for first order reaction.
  2. Nitrogen Pentoxide decomposes according to the equation given below:
    {tex}2{N_2}{O_5}(g) \to 4N{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g){/tex}
    This first order reaction was allowed to proceed at{tex} 40^0\;C{/tex} and the kinetic data are given below:
    [N2O5] [M] Time (min)
    0.400 0.00
    0.289 20.0
    0.209 40.0
    0.151 60.0
    0.109 80.0
     
    1. Calculate the rate constant.
    2. Predict the remaining concentration of {tex}N_2O_5{/tex} after 100 min?
    3. Calculate the initial rate of reaction.
question_text: Derive general form of the expression for half life period for first order reaction. Nitrogen Pentoxide decomposes according to the equation given below: {tex}2{N_2}{O_5}(g) \to 4N{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g){/tex} This first order reaction was allowed to proceed at {tex} 40^0\;C{/tex} and the kinetic data are given below: [N 2 O 5 ] [M] Time (min) 0.400 0.00 0.289 20.0 0.209 40.0 0.151 60.0 0.109 80.0 Calculate the rate constant. Predict the remaining concentration of {tex}N_2O_5{/tex} after 100 min? Calculate the initial rate of reaction.
explanation: For first order reaction {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} When {tex}\lbrack R\rbrack=\frac{{\left[R\right]}_0}2{/tex} , then t = t 1/2 {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_0}}}{{{{[R]}_0}/2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 2{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex} {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{k}{/tex} For first order reaction {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_0}}}{{\left[ R \right]}}{/tex} {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{20}}\log \left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{0.289}}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2.303}}{{20}}[\log 400 - \log 289]{/tex} k = 0.0163 min -1 {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}0.0163 = \frac{{2.303}}{100}\log \frac{{0.4}}{{[R]}}{/tex} [R] = 0.078 M Initial Rate = k[N 2 O 5 ] = 0.0163×0.40 = 6.52×10 -3 min -1
solution: For first order reaction {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} When {tex}\lbrack R\rbrack=\frac{{\left[R\right]}_0}2{/tex} , then t = t 1/2 {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_0}}}{{{{[R]}_0}/2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 2{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex} {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{k}{/tex} For first order reaction {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_0}}}{{\left[ R \right]}}{/tex} {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{20}}\log \left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{0.289}}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2.303}}{{20}}[\log 400 - \log 289]{/tex} k = 0.0163 min -1 {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}0.0163 = \frac{{2.303}}{100}\log \frac{{0.4}}{{[R]}}{/tex} [R] = 0.078 M Initial Rate = k[N 2 O 5 ] = 0.0163×0.40 = 6.52×10 -3 min -1
solution_html:
1. For first order reaction {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
    When {tex}\lbrack R\rbrack=\frac{{\left[R\right]}_0}2{/tex}, then t = t1/2
    {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_0}}}{{{{[R]}_0}/2}}{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 2{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex}
    {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{k}{/tex}
    1. For first order reaction
      {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_0}}}{{\left[ R \right]}}{/tex}
      {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{20}}\log \left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{0.289}}} \right){/tex}
      {tex}= \frac{{2.303}}{{20}}[\log 400 - \log 289]{/tex}
      k = 0.0163 min-1
    2. {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
      {tex}0.0163 = \frac{{2.303}}{100}\log \frac{{0.4}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
      [R] = 0.078 M
    3. Initial Rate = k[N2O5] = 0.0163×0.40 = 6.52×10-3 min-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The rate of most reaction double when their temperature is raised from 298K to 308K. Calculate activation energy of such a reaction. (R=8.314 J mol-1K-1, log 2 = 0.3010)
question_text: The rate of most reaction double when their temperature is raised from 298K to 308K. Calculate activation energy of such a reaction. (R=8.314 J mol -1 K -1 , log 2 = 0.3010)
explanation: {tex}\log \frac { k _ { 2 } } { k _ { 1 } } = \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { a } } { 2.303 R } \left( \frac { 1 } { T _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { T _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log 2 = \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { a } } { 2.303 \times 8.314 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 298 } - \frac { 1 } { 308 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0.3010 = \frac { E _ { a } } { 19.147 } \times \frac { 10 } { 298 \times 308 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { E } _ { a } = \frac { 0.301 \times 19.147 \times 298 \times 308 } { 10 \times 1000 } \mathrm { k } \mathrm { J } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { E } _ { a } = 52.987 \mathrm { kJ } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
solution: {tex}\log \frac { k _ { 2 } } { k _ { 1 } } = \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { a } } { 2.303 R } \left( \frac { 1 } { T _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { T _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log 2 = \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { a } } { 2.303 \times 8.314 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 298 } - \frac { 1 } { 308 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0.3010 = \frac { E _ { a } } { 19.147 } \times \frac { 10 } { 298 \times 308 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { E } _ { a } = \frac { 0.301 \times 19.147 \times 298 \times 308 } { 10 \times 1000 } \mathrm { k } \mathrm { J } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { E } _ { a } = 52.987 \mathrm { kJ } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\log \frac { k _ { 2 } } { k _ { 1 } } = \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { a } } { 2.303 R } \left( \frac { 1 } { T _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { T _ { 2 } } \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log 2 = \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { a } } { 2.303 \times 8.314 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 298 } - \frac { 1 } { 308 } \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 0.3010 = \frac { E _ { a } } { 19.147 } \times \frac { 10 } { 298 \times 308 }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { E } _ { a } = \frac { 0.301 \times 19.147 \times 298 \times 308 } { 10 \times 1000 } \mathrm { k } \mathrm { J } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { E } _ { a } = 52.987 \mathrm { kJ } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Define the following:

  1. Order of reaction
  2. Elementary step in a reaction.
question_text: Define the following: Order of reaction Elementary step in a reaction.
explanation: Order of a reaction : The sum of the powers of the concentration i.e. stoichiometric coefficients of reactants of a chemical reaction in rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. Each step of a complex reaction is called the elementary step of the reaction.
solution: Order of a reaction : The sum of the powers of the concentration i.e. stoichiometric coefficients of reactants of a chemical reaction in rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. Each step of a complex reaction is called the elementary step of the reaction.
solution_html:
1. Order of a reaction: The sum of the powers of the concentration i.e. stoichiometric coefficients of reactants of a chemical reaction in rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction.
  2. Each step of a complex reaction is called the elementary step of the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A reactant has a half-life of 10 min.

  1. Calculate the rate constant for the first order reaction.
  2. What fraction of the reactant will be left after an hour of the reaction has occurred?

subject: Chemistry
question_html: In a first order reaction, the concentration of the reactant is reduced from 0.6 mol L-1 to 0.2 mol L-1 in 5 min. Calculate the rate constant of the reaction.
question_text: In a first order reaction, the concentration of the reactant is reduced from 0.6 mol L -1 to 0.2 mol L -1 in 5 min. Calculate the rate constant of the reaction.
explanation: Rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} Given, [R]0 = 0.6 mol L -1 [R] = 0.2 mol L -1 , t = 5min {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log \frac{0.6}{0.2}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log 3{/tex} (log 3 = 0.4771) {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \times 0.4771{/tex} =0.2197min -1
solution: Rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} Given, [R]0 = 0.6 mol L -1 [R] = 0.2 mol L -1 , t = 5min {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log \frac{0.6}{0.2}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log 3{/tex} (log 3 = 0.4771) {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \times 0.4771{/tex} =0.2197min -1
solution_html:
Rate constant,
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
Given, [R]0 = 0.6 mol L-1
[R] = 0.2 mol L-1, t = 5min
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log \frac{0.6}{0.2}{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log 3{/tex} (log 3 = 0.4771)
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \times 0.4771{/tex}=0.2197min-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Discuss any four factors which affect the rate of a chemical reaction.
question_text: Discuss any four factors which affect the rate of a chemical reaction.
explanation: Factors influencing the rate of a chemical reaction are Nature of reactants- Different reactants require a different amount of energies for breaking the old bonds and for the formation of new bonds. Hence, the reactivity of a substance is related to the ease with which the specific bonds are broken or formed, e.g. 2NO + O 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO 2 (fast) 2CO + O 2 {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} 2CO 2 (slow) Concentration of reactants- The rate of reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of the reactants. Temperature- The rate of reaction increases with an increase in temperature. Catalyst- It alters the rate of the reaction without being consumed in the reaction. It provides an alternative path to the reaction with a low energy barrier.
solution: Factors influencing the rate of a chemical reaction are Nature of reactants- Different reactants require a different amount of energies for breaking the old bonds and for the formation of new bonds. Hence, the reactivity of a substance is related to the ease with which the specific bonds are broken or formed, e.g. 2NO + O 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO 2 (fast) 2CO + O 2 {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} 2CO 2 (slow) Concentration of reactants- The rate of reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of the reactants. Temperature- The rate of reaction increases with an increase in temperature. Catalyst- It alters the rate of the reaction without being consumed in the reaction. It provides an alternative path to the reaction with a low energy barrier.
solution_html:
Factors influencing the rate of a chemical reaction are 

  1. Nature of reactants- Different reactants require a different amount of energies for breaking the old bonds and for the formation of new bonds.
    Hence, the reactivity of a substance is related to the ease with which the specific bonds are broken or formed,
    e.g. 2NO + O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO2 (fast)
    2CO + O2 {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} 2CO2 (slow)
  2. Concentration of reactants- The rate of reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of the reactants.
  3. Temperature- The rate of reaction increases with an increase in temperature.
  4. Catalyst- It alters the rate of the reaction without being consumed in the reaction. It provides an alternative path to the reaction with a low energy barrier.

question_html: The rate of a reaction becomes four times when the temperature changes from 300 K to 320 K . Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction, assuming that it does not change with temperature. (R= 8. 314 J K-1mol-1)
question_text: The rate of a reaction becomes four times when the temperature changes from 300 K to 320 K . Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction, assuming that it does not change with temperature. (R= 8. 314 J K -1 mol -1 )
solution_html:
According to the given data:
{tex}\frac{k_2}{k_1}=4,T1=300K,T2=320K{/tex}
{tex}\frac{log k_2}{k_1}= \frac{E_a}{2.303×8.314JK^{−1}mol^{−1}}[\frac{320K−300K}{320K×300K}]{/tex}
{tex}0.6020=\frac{Ea×20}{2.303×8.314Jmol^{−1}×320×300}{/tex}
{tex}Ea=\frac{0.6020×2.303×8.314Jmol^{−1}×320×300}{20} {/tex}
{tex}=55327.6 \ Jmol^{−1}= 55.328 \ kJmol^{−1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The decomposition of phosphine, PH3 proceeds according to the following equation:
4PH3(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P4(g) + 6H2(g)
It is found that the reaction follows the following rate equation
Rate = k [PH3]
The half-life of PH3 is 37.9 s at 120oC.

  1. How much time is required for 3/4th of PH3 to decompose?
  2. What fraction of the original sample of PH3 remains behind after 1 min?
question_text: The decomposition of phosphine, PH 3 proceeds according to the following equation: 4PH 3 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P 4 (g) + 6H 2 (g) It is found that the reaction follows the following rate equation Rate = k [PH 3 ] The half-life of PH 3 is 37.9 s at 120 o C. How much time is required for 3/4th of PH 3 to decompose? What fraction of the original sample of PH 3 remains behind after 1 min?
explanation: Given, t 1/2 = 37.9s, initial concentration= [R] 0 concentration after time, {tex}t=\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}{/tex} (because {tex}\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{th}{/tex} of PH 3 is decomposed). {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{37.9} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{37.9}} \log 4{/tex} {tex}\text { or } t=\frac{2.303 \times 37.9 \times 0.6020}{0.693}=75.82 \mathrm{s}{/tex} t = 1 min = 60s and {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{37.9}=0.0183 \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}60=\frac{2.303}{0.0183} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} or {tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{60 \times 0.0183}{2.303}=0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\text { antilog } 0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=2.994{/tex} Let the initial amount is 100 {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{100}{2.994}=[R]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [R] = 33.40%
solution: Given, t 1/2 = 37.9s, initial concentration= [R] 0 concentration after time, {tex}t=\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}{/tex} (because {tex}\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{th}{/tex} of PH 3 is decomposed). {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{37.9} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{37.9}} \log 4{/tex} {tex}\text { or } t=\frac{2.303 \times 37.9 \times 0.6020}{0.693}=75.82 \mathrm{s}{/tex} t = 1 min = 60s and {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{37.9}=0.0183 \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}60=\frac{2.303}{0.0183} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} or {tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{60 \times 0.0183}{2.303}=0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\text { antilog } 0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=2.994{/tex} Let the initial amount is 100 {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{100}{2.994}=[R]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [R] = 33.40%
solution_html:
Given, t1/2 = 37.9s, initial concentration= [R]0
concentration after time, {tex}t=\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}{/tex}(because {tex}\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{th}{/tex} of PH3 is decomposed).

  1. {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{37.9} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{37.9}} \log 4{/tex}
    {tex}\text { or } t=\frac{2.303 \times 37.9 \times 0.6020}{0.693}=75.82 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
  2. t = 1 min = 60s and {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{37.9}=0.0183 \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    {tex}60=\frac{2.303}{0.0183} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    or {tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{60 \times 0.0183}{2.303}=0.4763{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\text { antilog } 0.4763{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=2.994{/tex}
    Let the initial amount is 100
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{100}{2.994}=[R]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [R] = 33.40%

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define rate of reaction?
question_text: Define rate of reaction?
explanation: Rate of reaction is defined as change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time. For example, the reaction A {tex}\to{/tex} B has the rate expressed as: rate of reaction {tex}=\frac{dx}{dt}=\frac{-[dA]}{dt} = \frac{[dB]}{dt}{/tex}
solution: Rate of reaction is defined as change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time. For example, the reaction A {tex}\to{/tex} B has the rate expressed as: rate of reaction {tex}=\frac{dx}{dt}=\frac{-[dA]}{dt} = \frac{[dB]}{dt}{/tex}
solution_html:
Rate of reaction is defined as change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time. For example, the reaction A{tex}\to{/tex}B has the rate expressed as:

rate of reaction {tex}=\frac{dx}{dt}=\frac{-[dA]}{dt} = \frac{[dB]}{dt}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Explain the difference between the instantaneous rate of a reaction and the average rate of a reaction.
question_text: Explain the difference between the instantaneous rate of a reaction and the average rate of a reaction.
explanation: Average rate (R av ) Instantaneous rate(R av ) i. Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in a specified time interval. i. Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product at particular moment of time. ii. It is concerned with the whole reaction process. ii. It is concerned with the particular moment of reaction. iii. Average rat = {tex}-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{R}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{P}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} iii. Instantaneous rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{P}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
solution: Average rate (R av ) Instantaneous rate(R av ) i. Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in a specified time interval. i. Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product at particular moment of time. ii. It is concerned with the whole reaction process. ii. It is concerned with the particular moment of reaction. iii. Average rat = {tex}-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{R}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{P}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} iii. Instantaneous rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{P}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Average rate (Rav) Instantaneous rate(Rav)
i. Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in a specified time interval. i. Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product at particular moment of time.
ii. It is concerned with the whole reaction process. ii. It is concerned with the particular moment of reaction.
iii. Average rat = {tex}-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{R}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{P}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} iii. Instantaneous rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{P}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The decomposition of a compound is found to follow at first order rate law. If it takes 15 minutes for 20 percent of original material to react, calculate:

  1. the rate constant.
  2. the time at which 10% of the original material remain unreacted.
question_text: The decomposition of a compound is found to follow at first order rate law. If it takes 15 minutes for 20 percent of original material to react, calculate: the rate constant. the time at which 10% of the original material remain unreacted.
explanation: 20% decomposition means [R] 0 = 100 M [R] = 100 - 20 = 80 M For first order reaction {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}}\log \frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex} {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}}\log (1.25) = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}} \times 0.0969{/tex} = 0.0148 min -1 the time at which 10% of the original material remain unreacted. {tex}0.0148 = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log 10{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.0148}} = 155.6\,\min{/tex}
solution: 20% decomposition means [R] 0 = 100 M [R] = 100 - 20 = 80 M For first order reaction {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}}\log \frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex} {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}}\log (1.25) = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}} \times 0.0969{/tex} = 0.0148 min -1 the time at which 10% of the original material remain unreacted. {tex}0.0148 = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log 10{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.0148}} = 155.6\,\min{/tex}
solution_html:
1. 20% decomposition means [R]0 = 100 M
    [R] = 100 - 20 = 80 M
    For first order reaction
    {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}}\log \frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex}
    {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}}\log (1.25) = \frac{{2.303}}{{15}} \times 0.0969{/tex}
    = 0.0148 min-1
  2. the time at which 10% of the original material remain unreacted.
    {tex}0.0148 = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log 10{/tex}
    {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.0148}} = 155.6\,\min{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The activation energy for the reaction
2 HI(g) {tex}\to{/tex} H2 + I2(g)
is 209.5 kJ mol-1 at 581 K. Calculate the fraction of molecules of reactants having energy equal to or greater than activation energy?
question_text: The activation energy for the reaction 2 HI(g) {tex}\to{/tex} H 2 + I 2 (g) is 209.5 kJ mol -1 at 581 K. Calculate the fraction of molecules of reactants having energy equal to or greater than activation energy?
explanation: Fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than activation energy. {tex} = n = \frac{n}{W} = {e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \log \,n\, = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex} .. or log n {tex} = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} . {tex} = - \frac{{209.5 \times {{10}^3}J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 581\,K}}{/tex} . {tex}= - 18.8323{/tex} . n = Anti log (-18.8323). = Antilog {tex}\overline {19} .1677{/tex} . {tex} = 1.471 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} .
solution: Fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than activation energy. {tex} = n = \frac{n}{W} = {e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \log \,n\, = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex} .. or log n {tex} = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} . {tex} = - \frac{{209.5 \times {{10}^3}J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 581\,K}}{/tex} . {tex}= - 18.8323{/tex} . n = Anti log (-18.8323). = Antilog {tex}\overline {19} .1677{/tex} . {tex} = 1.471 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than activation energy.
{tex} = n = \frac{n}{W} = {e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \log \,n\, = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex}..
or log n {tex} = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex}.
{tex} = - \frac{{209.5 \times {{10}^3}J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 581\,K}}{/tex}.
{tex}= - 18.8323{/tex}.
n = Anti log (-18.8323).
= Antilog {tex}\overline {19} .1677{/tex}.
{tex} = 1.471 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex}.

No. [A] [B] Initial rate of Formation of D 1. 0.1 M 0.1 M {tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 3}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} 2. 0.3 M 0.2 M {tex}7.2 \times {10^{ - 3}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} 3. 0.3 M 0.4 M {tex}2.88 \times {10^{ - 3}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} 4. 0.4 M 0.1 M {tex}2.40 \times {10^{ - 3}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} Determine rate law and the rate constant for the reaction.
