(iv) by (i) {tex}(4) = {(4)^a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \,\,a = 1{/tex} Divide eq. (iii) by (ii) {tex}4 = {(2)^b}{/tex} {tex}{2^2} = {(2)^b} \Rightarrow b = 2{/tex} Order with respect to A=1 Order with respect to B=2 {tex}Rate\,law\, = \frac{{d[D]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} On putting the value of 'a' and 'b' into any equation say (i) 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -3 M min -1 = k [0.1M] [0.1M] 2 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -3 M min-1 = k [0.1 M] 3 k= 6.0 M -2 min -1
solution: For the reaction 2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D Let rate law expression as Rate = {tex}k{[A]^a}{[B]^b}{/tex} According to question {tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{(0.1)^a}{(0.1)^b}....(i){/tex} {tex}7.2 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{(0.3)^a}{(0.2)^b}....(ii){/tex} {tex}2.88 \times {10^{ - 2}} = k{(0.3)^a}{(0.4)^b}....(iii){/tex} {tex}2.40 \times {10^{ - 2}} = k{(0.4)^a}{(0.1)^b}....(iv){/tex} Divide eq. (iv) by (i) {tex}(4) = {(4)^a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \,\,a = 1{/tex} Divide eq. (iii) by (ii) {tex}4 = {(2)^b}{/tex} {tex}{2^2} = {(2)^b} \Rightarrow b = 2{/tex} Order with respect to A=1 Order with respect to B=2 {tex}Rate\,law\, = \frac{{d[D]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} On putting the value of 'a' and 'b' into any equation say (i) 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -3 M min -1 = k [0.1M] [0.1M] 2 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -3 M min-1 = k [0.1 M] 3 k= 6.0 M -2 min -1
solution_html:
For the reaction 2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D
Let rate law expression as
Rate = {tex}k{[A]^a}{[B]^b}{/tex}
According to question
{tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{(0.1)^a}{(0.1)^b}....(i){/tex}
{tex}7.2 \times {10^{ - 3}} = k{(0.3)^a}{(0.2)^b}....(ii){/tex}
{tex}2.88 \times {10^{ - 2}} = k{(0.3)^a}{(0.4)^b}....(iii){/tex}
{tex}2.40 \times {10^{ - 2}} = k{(0.4)^a}{(0.1)^b}....(iv){/tex}
Divide eq. (iv) by (i)
{tex}(4) = {(4)^a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \,\,a = 1{/tex}
Divide eq. (iii) by (ii)
{tex}4 = {(2)^b}{/tex}
{tex}{2^2} = {(2)^b} \Rightarrow b = 2{/tex}
Order with respect to A=1
Order with respect to B=2
{tex}Rate\,law\, = \frac{{d[D]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
On putting the value of 'a' and 'b' into any equation say (i)

6.0 {tex} \times {/tex}10-3 M min-1 = k [0.1M] [0.1M]2

6.0 {tex} \times {/tex}10-3 M min-1 = k [0.1 M]3

k= 6.0 M-2 min-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
In a pseudo first order hydrolysis of ester in water, the following results were obtained:

t/s 0 30 60 90
[Ester] molL-1 0.55 0.31 0.17 0.085
  1. Calculate the average rate of reaction between the time interval 30 to 60 seconds.
  2. Calculate the pseudo first order rate constant for the hydrolysis of ester.
question_text: In a pseudo first order hydrolysis of ester in water, the following results were obtained: t/s 0 30 60 90 [Ester] molL -1 0.55 0.31 0.17 0.085 Calculate the average rate of reaction between the time interval 30 to 60 seconds. Calculate the pseudo first order rate constant for the hydrolysis of ester.
explanation: Average rate of reaction between the time interval, 30 to 60 seconds, {tex}=\frac{d[Ester]}{dt}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{0.31-0.17}{60-30} =\frac{0.14}{30} =4.67\times {{10}^{-3}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} For a pseudo first order reaction, {tex} k=\frac{2.303}{t}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]} {/tex} For t = 30s, {tex} {{k}_{1}}=\frac{2.303}{30}\log \frac{0.55}{0.31} =1.911\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} For t = 60s, {tex} {{k}_{2}}=\frac{2.303}{60}\log \frac{0.55}{0.17} = 1.957\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} For t = 90s, {tex}{{k}_{3}}=\frac{2.303}{90}\log \frac{0.55}{0.085} =2.075\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} Then, average rate constant, {tex} k=\frac{{{k}_{1}}+{{k}_{2}}+{{k}_{3}}}{3} {/tex} = 1.981 X 10 -2 s -1
solution: Average rate of reaction between the time interval, 30 to 60 seconds, {tex}=\frac{d[Ester]}{dt}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{0.31-0.17}{60-30} =\frac{0.14}{30} =4.67\times {{10}^{-3}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} For a pseudo first order reaction, {tex} k=\frac{2.303}{t}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]} {/tex} For t = 30s, {tex} {{k}_{1}}=\frac{2.303}{30}\log \frac{0.55}{0.31} =1.911\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} For t = 60s, {tex} {{k}_{2}}=\frac{2.303}{60}\log \frac{0.55}{0.17} = 1.957\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} For t = 90s, {tex}{{k}_{3}}=\frac{2.303}{90}\log \frac{0.55}{0.085} =2.075\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex} Then, average rate constant, {tex} k=\frac{{{k}_{1}}+{{k}_{2}}+{{k}_{3}}}{3} {/tex} = 1.981 X 10 -2 s -1
solution_html:
1. Average rate of reaction between the time interval, 30 to 60 seconds,{tex}=\frac{d[Ester]}{dt}{/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{0.31-0.17}{60-30} =\frac{0.14}{30} =4.67\times {{10}^{-3}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex}
  2. For a pseudo first order reaction,
    {tex} k=\frac{2.303}{t}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]} {/tex}
    For t= 30s,{tex} {{k}_{1}}=\frac{2.303}{30}\log \frac{0.55}{0.31} =1.911\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex}
    For t= 60s, {tex} {{k}_{2}}=\frac{2.303}{60}\log \frac{0.55}{0.17} = 1.957\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex}
    For t= 90s,
    {tex}{{k}_{3}}=\frac{2.303}{90}\log \frac{0.55}{0.085} =2.075\times {{10}^{-2}}{{s}^{-1}} {/tex}
    Then, average rate constant,{tex} k=\frac{{{k}_{1}}+{{k}_{2}}+{{k}_{3}}}{3} {/tex}= 1.981 X 10-2 s-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A reaction is of first order in reactant A and of second order in reactant B. How is the rate of this reaction affected when

  1. the concentration of B alone is increased to three times?
  2. the concentrations of A as well as B are doubled?
question_text: A reaction is of first order in reactant A and of second order in reactant B. How is the rate of this reaction affected when the concentration of B alone is increased to three times? the concentrations of A as well as B are doubled?
explanation: Since, the reaction is of first order wrt A and second order wrt B, then the rate law can be given as, (Rate) 1 =k [A] [B] 2 When the concentration of B is increased to three times (3B), the rate would be (Rate) 2 =k [A] [3B] 2 (Rate) 2 = 9 k [A] [B] 2 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} (Rate}, {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate is increased by 9 times. (Rate) 3 = k [2A] [2B] 2 (Rate) 3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} k [A] [B] 2 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} (Rate) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate is increased by 8 times
solution: Since, the reaction is of first order wrt A and second order wrt B, then the rate law can be given as, (Rate) 1 =k [A] [B] 2 When the concentration of B is increased to three times (3B), the rate would be (Rate) 2 =k [A] [3B] 2 (Rate) 2 = 9 k [A] [B] 2 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} (Rate}, {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate is increased by 9 times. (Rate) 3 = k [2A] [2B] 2 (Rate) 3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} k [A] [B] 2 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} (Rate) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate is increased by 8 times
solution_html:
Since, the reaction is of first order wrt A and second order wrt B, then the rate law can be given as,
(Rate)1 =k [A] [B]2

  1. When the concentration of B is increased to three times (3B), the rate would be
    (Rate)2 =k [A] [3B]2
    (Rate)2 = 9 k [A] [B]2 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} (Rate},
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate is increased by 9 times.
  2. (Rate)3 = k [2A] [2B]2
    (Rate)3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} k [A] [B]2= 8 {tex}\times{/tex} (Rate)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate is increased by 8 times

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Show that for a first order reaction, the time required for half the change (half-life period) is independent of initial concentration.
question_text: Show that for a first order reaction, the time required for half the change (half-life period) is independent of initial concentration.
explanation: For a first order reaction, rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} When {tex}t=t_{1 / 2^{\prime}}[R]=\frac{[R]_{0}}{2}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{2303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]_{0} / 2}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{2303}{k} \log 2=\frac{2303 \times 03010}{k}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0693}{k}{/tex} For a first order reaction, half-life period is constant. It is independent of initial concentration of the reacting species.
solution: For a first order reaction, rate constant, {tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} When {tex}t=t_{1 / 2^{\prime}}[R]=\frac{[R]_{0}}{2}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{2303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]_{0} / 2}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{2303}{k} \log 2=\frac{2303 \times 03010}{k}{/tex} {tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0693}{k}{/tex} For a first order reaction, half-life period is constant. It is independent of initial concentration of the reacting species.
solution_html:
For a first order reaction, rate constant,
{tex}k=\frac{2303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
When {tex}t=t_{1 / 2^{\prime}}[R]=\frac{[R]_{0}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{2303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]_{0} / 2}{/tex}
{tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{2303}{k} \log 2=\frac{2303 \times 03010}{k}{/tex}
{tex}t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0693}{k}{/tex}
For a first order reaction, half-life period is constant. It is independent of initial concentration of the reacting species.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Define the following.

  1. Elementary step in a reaction
  2. Rate of a reaction
question_text: Define the following. Elementary step in a reaction Rate of a reaction
explanation: Each step of a complex reaction is called the elementary step of the reaction. Rate of a reaction : Change in concentration of reactants or products in unit time is known as rate of a reaction. Its unit is mol L -1 s -1 .
solution: Each step of a complex reaction is called the elementary step of the reaction. Rate of a reaction : Change in concentration of reactants or products in unit time is known as rate of a reaction. Its unit is mol L -1 s -1 .
solution_html:
1. Each step of a complex reaction is called the elementary step of the reaction.
  2. Rate of a reaction: Change in concentration of reactants or products in unit time is known as rate of a reaction. Its unit is mol L-1 s-1.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Explain the term order of reaction. Derive the unit for first order rate constant.
question_text: Explain the term order of reaction. Derive the unit for first order rate constant.
explanation: Order of a reaction: The sum of the powers of the concentration i.e. stoichiometric coefficients of reactants of a chemical reaction in rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. For example, for the reaction given below: aA + bB {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C Rate= k [A] x [B] y Order of reaction = x + y Where x and y are actual stoichiometric coefficients for the reaction. Unit of rate constant for first order, {tex}\text { Rate }=-\frac{d[A]}{d t}=k[A]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\operatorname{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{\mathrm{s}}=k\left(\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}\right){/tex} k = s -1
solution: Order of a reaction: The sum of the powers of the concentration i.e. stoichiometric coefficients of reactants of a chemical reaction in rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. For example, for the reaction given below: aA + bB {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C Rate= k [A] x [B] y Order of reaction = x + y Where x and y are actual stoichiometric coefficients for the reaction. Unit of rate constant for first order, {tex}\text { Rate }=-\frac{d[A]}{d t}=k[A]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\operatorname{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{\mathrm{s}}=k\left(\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}\right){/tex} k = s -1
solution_html:
Order of a reaction: The sum of the powers of the concentration i.e. stoichiometric coefficients of reactants of a chemical reaction in rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. For example, for the reaction given below:
aA + bB{tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}C
Rate= k [A]x [B]y
Order of reaction = x + y
Where x and y are actual stoichiometric coefficients for the reaction.
Unit of rate constant for first order,
{tex}\text { Rate }=-\frac{d[A]}{d t}=k[A]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\operatorname{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{\mathrm{s}}=k\left(\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}\right){/tex}
k = s-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The half-life for a first order reaction is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 s. What percentage of the initial reactant will react in 2h?
question_text: The half-life for a first order reaction is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 s. What percentage of the initial reactant will react in 2h?
explanation: First, find the value of k by using formula, {tex}k=\frac{0693}{t_{1 / 2}}{/tex} , then the value of {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{R}]}{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{/tex} Given , t 1/2 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 s and t = 2h = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60s {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{4}}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{4}}=\frac{2.303}{2 \times 60 \times 60} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{0.693 \times 2 \times 60 \times 60}{5 \times 10^{4} \times 2.303}=0.0433{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\operatorname{antilog}(0.0433){/tex} = antilog(0.0433) {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=1.105 \Rightarrow \frac{[R]}{[R]_{0}}=\frac{1 \times 100}{1.105} \%=90.497 \%{/tex}
solution: First, find the value of k by using formula, {tex}k=\frac{0693}{t_{1 / 2}}{/tex} , then the value of {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{R}]}{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{/tex} Given , t 1/2 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 s and t = 2h = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60s {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{4}}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{4}}=\frac{2.303}{2 \times 60 \times 60} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{0.693 \times 2 \times 60 \times 60}{5 \times 10^{4} \times 2.303}=0.0433{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\operatorname{antilog}(0.0433){/tex} = antilog(0.0433) {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=1.105 \Rightarrow \frac{[R]}{[R]_{0}}=\frac{1 \times 100}{1.105} \%=90.497 \%{/tex}
solution_html:
First, find the value of k by using formula,
{tex}k=\frac{0693}{t_{1 / 2}}{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{R}]}{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{/tex}
Given , t1/2 = 5{tex}\times{/tex}104 s and t = 2h = 2{tex}\times{/tex}60{tex}\times{/tex}60s
{tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{4}}{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{4}}=\frac{2.303}{2 \times 60 \times 60} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{0.693 \times 2 \times 60 \times 60}{5 \times 10^{4} \times 2.303}=0.0433{/tex}
{tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\operatorname{antilog}(0.0433){/tex} = antilog(0.0433)
{tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=1.105 \Rightarrow \frac{[R]}{[R]_{0}}=\frac{1 \times 100}{1.105} \%=90.497 \%{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A first order reaction is 20% completed in 5 min. Calculate the time taken for the reaction to be 60% complete.
question_text: A first order reaction is 20% completed in 5 min. Calculate the time taken for the reaction to be 60% complete.
explanation: When a first order reaction is 20% completed in 5 min, [R]0 = 100 M, [R] = 100 - 20 = 80 M and t = 5 min {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log \frac{100}{80}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log 1.25{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \times 0.0969=0.0446 \mathrm{min}^{-1}{/tex} When the reaction is 60% completed, [R] 0 =100 M and [R] = 100- 60 = 40 M k = 0.0446 min -1 Time taken to complete 60% reaction. {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{0.0446} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{0.0446} \log 2.5=\frac{2.303 \times 0.3979}{0.0446}{/tex} = 20.546
solution: When a first order reaction is 20% completed in 5 min, [R]0 = 100 M, [R] = 100 - 20 = 80 M and t = 5 min {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log \frac{100}{80}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log 1.25{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \times 0.0969=0.0446 \mathrm{min}^{-1}{/tex} When the reaction is 60% completed, [R] 0 =100 M and [R] = 100- 60 = 40 M k = 0.0446 min -1 Time taken to complete 60% reaction. {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{0.0446} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{0.0446} \log 2.5=\frac{2.303 \times 0.3979}{0.0446}{/tex} = 20.546
solution_html:
When a first order reaction is 20% completed in
5 min, [R]0 = 100 M, [R] = 100 - 20 = 80 M
and t = 5 min
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log \frac{100}{80}{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \log 1.25{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{5} \times 0.0969=0.0446 \mathrm{min}^{-1}{/tex}
When the reaction is 60% completed,
[R]0=100 M and [R] = 100- 60 = 40 M
k = 0.0446 min-1
Time taken to complete 60% reaction.
{tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
{tex}t=\frac{2.303}{0.0446} \log \frac{100}{40}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{0.0446} \log 2.5=\frac{2.303 \times 0.3979}{0.0446}{/tex}
= 20.546

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The data given below is for the reaction,
{tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {2{N_2}{O_5}(g) \to 4N{0_2}(g){\rm{ + }}{O_2}(g)} \end{array}{/tex}

S. No. N2O5 (mol L-1) Rate of disappearance of N2O5 (mol L-1 min-1)
1. 1.13 × 10-2 34 ×10-5
2. 0.84 × 10-2 25 × 10-5
3. 0.62 × 10-2 18 × 10-5

Determine for this reaction,

  1. Order of reaction
  2. Rate law
  3. Rate constant
question_text: The data given below is for the reaction, {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {2{N_2}{O_5}(g) \to 4N{0_2}(g){\rm{ + }}{O_2}(g)} \end{array}{/tex} S. No. N 2 O 5 (mol L -1 ) Rate of disappearance of N 2 O 5 (mol L -1 min -1 ) 1. 1.13 × 10 -2 34 ×10 -5 2. 0.84 × 10 -2 25 × 10 -5 3. 0.62 × 10 -2 18 × 10 -5 Determine for this reaction, Order of reaction Rate law Rate constant
explanation: For the reaction {tex} 2{N_2}{O_5}(g) \to 4N{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g){/tex} Assume rate law expression as Rate = {tex}k{[{N_2}{O_5}]^x}{/tex} According to question {tex}34 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k[1.13 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x{/tex} ... (i) {tex}25 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{[0.84 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}18 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{[0.62 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x}{/tex} ...(iii) From eq.(i) and eq.(ii) {tex}\frac{{34 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = \frac{{k{{[1.13 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}]}^x}}}{{k{{[0.84{\kern 1pt} \times {{10}^{ - 2}}]}^x}}}{/tex} {tex}[1.36] = {[1.36]^x}{/tex} x = 1 Order of reaction=1 Rate law expression Rate = {tex}k[{N_2}{O_5}]{/tex} Rate constant {tex}k = \frac{{[Rate]}}{{[{N_2}{O_5}]}} = \frac{{34 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{1.13 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{/tex} {tex}k = 30.09 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} {tex}k = 3.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution: For the reaction {tex} 2{N_2}{O_5}(g) \to 4N{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g){/tex} Assume rate law expression as Rate = {tex}k{[{N_2}{O_5}]^x}{/tex} According to question {tex}34 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k[1.13 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x{/tex} ... (i) {tex}25 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{[0.84 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}18 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{[0.62 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x}{/tex} ...(iii) From eq.(i) and eq.(ii) {tex}\frac{{34 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = \frac{{k{{[1.13 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}]}^x}}}{{k{{[0.84{\kern 1pt} \times {{10}^{ - 2}}]}^x}}}{/tex} {tex}[1.36] = {[1.36]^x}{/tex} x = 1 Order of reaction=1 Rate law expression Rate = {tex}k[{N_2}{O_5}]{/tex} Rate constant {tex}k = \frac{{[Rate]}}{{[{N_2}{O_5}]}} = \frac{{34 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{1.13 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{/tex} {tex}k = 30.09 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} {tex}k = 3.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
For the reaction {tex} 2{N_2}{O_5}(g) \to 4N{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g){/tex}
Assume rate law expression as
Rate = {tex}k{[{N_2}{O_5}]^x}{/tex}
According to question
{tex}34 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k[1.13 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x{/tex}... (i)
{tex}25 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{[0.84 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x}{/tex}...(ii)
{tex}18 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{[0.62 \times {10^{ - 2}}]^x}{/tex}...(iii)
From eq.(i) and eq.(ii)
{tex}\frac{{34 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = \frac{{k{{[1.13 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}]}^x}}}{{k{{[0.84{\kern 1pt} \times {{10}^{ - 2}}]}^x}}}{/tex}
{tex}[1.36] = {[1.36]^x}{/tex}
x = 1

  1. Order of reaction=1
  2. Rate law expression
    Rate = {tex}k[{N_2}{O_5}]{/tex}
  3. Rate constant
    {tex}k = \frac{{[Rate]}}{{[{N_2}{O_5}]}} = \frac{{34 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{1.13 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{/tex}
    {tex}k = 30.09 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex}
    {tex}k = 3.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The half life for radioactive decay of 14C is 5730 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 80% of the 14C found in a living tree. Estimate the age of the sample.
question_text: The half life for radioactive decay of 14 C is 5730 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 80% of the 14 C found in a living tree. Estimate the age of the sample.
explanation: Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics, Decay constant (K) {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex} = 1845 years
solution: Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics, Decay constant (K) {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex} = 1845 years
solution_html:
Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics,
Decay constant (K)  {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex}
= 1845 years

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
For a chemical reaction, what is the effect of a catalyst on the following?

  1. Activation energy of the reaction. 
  2. Rate of the reaction.
question_text: For a chemical reaction, what is the effect of a catalyst on the following? Activation energy of the reaction. Rate of the reaction.
explanation: A catalyst decreases the activation energy of the reaction. It provides an alternative path to the reaction. A catalyst increases the rate of a reaction by decreasing the activation energy.
solution: A catalyst decreases the activation energy of the reaction. It provides an alternative path to the reaction. A catalyst increases the rate of a reaction by decreasing the activation energy.
solution_html:
1. A catalyst decreases the activation energy of the reaction. It provides an alternative path to the reaction.
  2. A catalyst increases the rate of a reaction by decreasing the activation energy.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The decomposition of phosphine,
4PH3 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P4|(g) + 6H2 (g)
has the rate law, rate = k [PH3]
The rate constant is 6. 0{tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 s-1 at 300 K and activation energy is 3. 05 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J mol-1. Calculate the value of rate constant at 310 K.
(Given, R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1)
question_text: The decomposition of phosphine, 4PH 3 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P 4 |(g) + 6H 2 (g) has the rate law, rate = k [PH 3 ] The rate constant is 6. 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 s -1 at 300 K and activation energy is 3. 05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J mol -1 . Calculate the value of rate constant at 310 K. (Given, R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 )
explanation: Given, T 1 = 300K, T 2 = 310K, E a = 3.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J mol -1 k 1 = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 s -1 , k 2 = ? Using Arrhenius equation, {tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{3.05 \times 10^{5}}{2.303 \times 8314}\left(\frac{310-300}{300 \times 310}\right){/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{3.05 \times 10^{5} \times 10}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 310}=1.7128{/tex} {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}{/tex} = antilog (1.7128) {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=5.162 \times 10^{1}=51.62{/tex} {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}=51.62{/tex} or k 2 = 3.0972 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s -1
solution: Given, T 1 = 300K, T 2 = 310K, E a = 3.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J mol -1 k 1 = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 s -1 , k 2 = ? Using Arrhenius equation, {tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{3.05 \times 10^{5}}{2.303 \times 8314}\left(\frac{310-300}{300 \times 310}\right){/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{3.05 \times 10^{5} \times 10}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 310}=1.7128{/tex} {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}{/tex} = antilog (1.7128) {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=5.162 \times 10^{1}=51.62{/tex} {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}=51.62{/tex} or k 2 = 3.0972 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s -1
solution_html:
Given,
T1 = 300K, T2 = 310K,
Ea = 3.05 {tex}\times{/tex}105 J mol-1
k1 = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 s-1, k2 = ?
Using Arrhenius equation,
{tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{3.05 \times 10^{5}}{2.303 \times 8314}\left(\frac{310-300}{300 \times 310}\right){/tex}
{tex}\log \left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{3.05 \times 10^{5} \times 10}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 310}=1.7128{/tex}
{tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}{/tex} = antilog (1.7128)
{tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=5.162 \times 10^{1}=51.62{/tex}
{tex}\frac{k_{2}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}=51.62{/tex} or k2  = 3.0972 {tex}\times{/tex}10-2 s-1
